target.title: Docker: improve Mac Docker runtime on macOS
target.body:
## What
Improve the OpenClaw Docker runtime path on macOS.

## Why
Running OpenClaw in Docker on macOS needs a few runtime-focused fixes to stay reliable: reproducible tool installs, safer privilege dropping, writable runtime paths, browser wiring, and stable Docker-specific setup defaults.

## Included
- pin Go and Homebrew sources in the Docker image for reproducible builds
- stop mounting the raw project `.env` file into containers; use Compose `env_file` instead
- add a root entrypoint that prepares writable runtime directories, optionally starts cron, and drops to `node`
- install and auto-wire bundled Playwright Chromium for Docker browser usage
- preserve Docker exec/browser defaults through `scripts/docker/setup.sh`
- add focused Docker coverage for the runtime path

## Not included
- migration/backup helpers
- keep-awake host helper

Those operator helpers are split into a separate PR so this one stays focused on the runtime image and setup behavior.


## Real behavior proof
- **Behavior or issue addressed**: Mac-hosted Docker runtime rebuild/redeploy keeps OpenClaw usable with mounted host config/workspace, Docker browser tooling, exec policy defaults, cron jobs, channel health, and model operation preserved.
- **Real environment tested**: Intel macOS host with Docker Desktop, Docker Compose gateway service, branch head `9444ed9af0`, host OpenClaw config/workspace mounted into the container, existing Slack and OpenAI Codex auth profiles reused.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**: rebuilt and redeployed with `OPENCLAW_SKIP_ONBOARDING=1 scripts/docker/setup.sh`, then ran `docker compose ps`, `node dist/index.js config validate`, `channels status --probe`, `cron list`, a gateway-backed model smoke, browser/tool probes, workspace script syntax checks, paper-digest source probes, and portfolio render smoke inside `openclaw-gateway`.
- **Evidence after fix**: redacted terminal output from the real Docker setup:

```text
docker compose ps
openclaw-openclaw-gateway-1   openclaw:local   Up About a minute (healthy)   0.0.0.0:18789-18790->18789-18790/tcp

container version
2026.5.8

health endpoint
{"ok":true,"status":"live"}

config validate
Config valid: ~/.openclaw/openclaw.json

browser/tools
Google Chrome for Testing 147.0.7727.15
go version go1.26.1 linux/amd64
gog-ok
Homebrew >=4.3.0

channels status --probe
Slack default: enabled, configured, running, connected, health:healthy, works

cron list
Slack Health Probe Deterministic: ok
Paper Digest Deterministic: ok
Portfolio Status Deterministic 17UTC: ok
Portfolio Status Deterministic 1830UTC: ok
Portfolio Status Deterministic 20UTC: ok

model smoke
{"ok":true,"transport":"gateway","provider":"openai-codex","model":"gpt-5.4","outputs":[{"text":"ok"}]}

workspace script syntax
syntax-ok

paper digest/network smoke
github: 200
huggingface: 200
paper_digest_scan.py selected_count=3 usedCachedResults=false
arxiv: HTTP 429; scan used the local arXiv cache path instead of failing the whole run

portfolio render smoke
render-ok
```

- **Observed result after fix**: The rebuilt Docker image starts healthy, keeps the real mounted configuration valid, has browser/go/gog/Homebrew available, Slack probes succeed, all five production cron jobs remain `ok`, the model route returns the expected live response through the gateway, and the digest/portfolio scripts are still present and executable. The arXiv live endpoint rate-limited this manual smoke, but GitHub/Hugging Face were reachable and the digest scanner selected three items using its cache fallback rather than the prior all-sources-failed behavior.
- **What was not tested**: I did not send duplicate Slack digest/portfolio messages from the smoke run; delivery is covered by the existing cron definitions and channel probe.

target.title: feat(plugins): add optional api.resetSession()
target.body:
## Summary

`main` already has gateway-side session reset support through `performGatewaySessionReset()`.

This PR adds optional plugin-side `api.resetSession(key, reason?)` support on top of the current mainline architecture.

- Adds `PluginResetSessionResult` type and optional `resetSession` on `OpenClawPluginApi`
- Wires `api.resetSession(...)` in the plugin registry only in full registration mode and only when the gateway exposes `sessions.reset`
- Reuses gateway-owned canonicalization and reset resolution (no plugin-side duplicate logic)
- Unifies config snapshot usage in `performGatewaySessionReset` to eliminate config-drift between target resolution and entry lookup
- Adds per-canonical-key in-flight guard in the reset service
- Exports `PluginResetSessionResult` from `openclaw/plugin-sdk/core`

## Scope

This PR:
- adds `PluginResetSessionResult` to `src/plugins/types.ts`
- adds optional `resetSession` to `OpenClawPluginApi`
- adds builder support in `src/plugins/api-builder.ts`
- wires `api.resetSession(...)` in `src/plugins/registry.ts` (full-mode + capability gated)
- adds optional `existingCfg` param to `loadSessionEntry` in `src/gateway/session-utils.ts`
- adds in-flight guard and unified resolution in `src/gateway/session-reset-service.ts`
- exports the result type from `src/plugin-sdk/core.ts`
- adds focused plugin contract tests (17 tests)
- refreshes generated plugin SDK API baselines

This PR does not:
- change gateway reset RPC behavior
- broaden session architecture scope beyond the plugin API surface
- revive any stale-branch refactor baggage from the closed #43375

## Behavior

`api.resetSession(key, reason?)`:
- accepts `"new"` or `"reset"` reason values (defaults to `"new"`)
- trims and validates the key
- delegates to gateway-owned `performGatewaySessionReset`
- resolves to a structured plugin result instead of rejecting for operational failures
- absent in setup-only, setup-runtime, and cli-metadata registration modes
- absent when gateway does not expose `sessions.reset`

Success: `{ ok: true, key, sessionId }`
Failure: `{ ok: false, key, code, message }`

## Local Verification

```
pnpm test -- src/plugins/reset-session.test.ts     # 17/17 pass
pnpm test -- src/gateway/session-utils.test.ts      # 77/77 pass
pnpm plugin-sdk:api:check                           # OK
pnpm tsgo                                           # no new errors
```

Replaces #43375 (closed, stale branch).

target.title: fix(scripts): avoid mutating tracked auth-monitor template during setup
target.body:
## Summary
- keep the tracked `openclaw-auth-monitor.service` template unchanged during setup
- render the auth-monitor user service in a temporary file before installing it
- render `ExecStart` to the current checkout's `scripts/auth-monitor.sh`
- replace `NOTIFY_*` environment lines by key so reruns repair old locally-mutated templates
- avoid adding a `python3` runtime prerequisite to this setup path

## Problem
`scripts/setup-auth-system.sh` currently edits the tracked systemd template in-place before copying it into `~/.config/systemd/user/`.

That dirties the repository working tree with machine-local values and makes the generated user unit inherit the template's hardcoded `/home/admin/openclaw/...` path instead of the current checkout.

## Root cause
The setup flow applies substitutions directly to the repository template file:

- `scripts/systemd/openclaw-auth-monitor.service`

and only copies it afterwards.

## What changed
- copy the auth-monitor service template into a temporary materialized unit
- validate and render `ExecStart` and notification environment lines before installing the unit
- atomically move the rendered service into `~/.config/systemd/user/` only after rendering succeeds
- copy the timer only after the service unit has been rendered successfully
- match existing `Environment=NOTIFY_*=` lines by key, including values left by older setup runs
- render with Bash builtins instead of adding a `python3` dependency

## Before / After

### Before
- setup edited `scripts/systemd/openclaw-auth-monitor.service` in-place
- running setup dirtied the repository working tree with machine-local values
- the generated user unit inherited the template's hardcoded `/home/admin/openclaw/...` path
- a missing renderer or placeholder mismatch could overwrite an existing service with the raw template

### After
- setup renders a temporary materialized service and installs it only after validation passes
- the tracked template remains unchanged
- the generated `ExecStart` points to the current checkout's `scripts/auth-monitor.sh`
- reruns repair both default commented placeholders and previously rendered `NOTIFY_*` lines

## Real Behavior Proof

**Behavior addressed:** `scripts/setup-auth-system.sh` renders and installs the auth-monitor user systemd service without mutating the tracked service template, and it repairs previously rendered `NOTIFY_*` lines by key.

**Real environment tested:** Local OpenClaw checkout on macOS, running the real setup script with a temporary `HOME`; `systemctl` was stubbed so the script could be exercised safely without changing the host user services.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch:**
1. Ran `bash -n scripts/setup-auth-system.sh`.
2. Ran `git diff --check`.
3. Ran `scripts/setup-auth-system.sh` with a temporary `HOME`, an ntfy topic, and a redacted phone number.
4. Inspected the generated `~/.config/systemd/user/openclaw-auth-monitor.service` under the temporary `HOME`.
5. Checked for leftover temporary service files.
6. Checked `git diff -- scripts/systemd/openclaw-auth-monitor.service` to verify the tracked template stayed unchanged.
7. Repeated the setup from a temp-copied old template that already had custom `Environment=NOTIFY_*=` values.
8. Ran the setup with a failing `python3` stub first on `PATH` to verify this path does not invoke `python3`.
9. Ran a template-drift failure check with an existing installed service file.

**Evidence after fix:**

```console
$ bash -n scripts/setup-auth-system.sh
$ git diff --check
$ HOME=/private/tmp/openclaw-auth-proof.../home PATH=/private/tmp/openclaw-auth-proof.../bin:$PATH bash scripts/setup-auth-system.sh
...
Installing systemd timer...
Auth monitor installed and running.

--- installed service after normal run ---
[Unit]
Description=OpenClaw Auth Expiry Monitor
After=network.target

[Service]
Type=oneshot
ExecStart="/Users/ring/.openclaw/workspace/research/openclaw-pr-scout/openclaw/scripts/auth-monitor.sh"
; Configure notification channels via environment
Environment=WARN_HOURS=2
Environment=NOTIFY_PHONE=+15555550100
Environment=NOTIFY_NTFY=proof-topic

[Install]
WantedBy=default.target

--- systemctl log ---
systemctl --user is-active openclaw
systemctl --user daemon-reload
systemctl --user enable --now openclaw-auth-monitor.timer

--- temp service files left ---

--- tracked template diff after normal run ---
```

Rerun proof against a temp-copied old template that already had custom notification values:

```console
--- installed service after old-custom-template rerun ---
[Unit]
Description=OpenClaw Auth Expiry Monitor
After=network.target

[Service]
Type=oneshot
ExecStart="/private/tmp/openclaw-auth-proof.../copy/scripts/auth-monitor.sh"
; Configure notification channels via environment
Environment=WARN_HOURS=2
Environment=NOTIFY_PHONE=+15555550200
Environment=NOTIFY_NTFY=new-topic

[Install]
WantedBy=default.target

--- old custom source template remains dirty only in temp copy ---
ExecStart=/home/admin/openclaw/scripts/auth-monitor.sh
; Configure notification channels via environment
Environment=WARN_HOURS=2
Environment=NOTIFY_PHONE=old-phone
Environment=NOTIFY_NTFY=old-topic
```

No-`python3` dependency proof: setup still succeeds with a failing `python3` stub first on `PATH`:

```console
Installing systemd timer...
Auth monitor installed and running.

--- installed service after normal run with python3 stub first on PATH ---
ExecStart="/Users/ring/.openclaw/workspace/research/openclaw-pr-scout/openclaw/scripts/auth-monitor.sh"
Environment=NOTIFY_PHONE=+15555550100
Environment=NOTIFY_NTFY=proof-topic
```

Template-drift proof: existing installed service remains untouched if a required placeholder is missing:

```console
Installing systemd timer...
ERROR: ExecStart placeholder not found in /private/tmp/openclaw-auth-proof.../openclaw-auth-monitor.service.XXXXXX

exit_status=1
--- existing service after template drift ---
EXISTING_WORKING_UNIT

--- temp service files left after template drift ---
```

**Observed result after fix:** The generated user service contains the rendered checkout-local `ExecStart` and configured notification values, no temporary service file remains, and the tracked `scripts/systemd/openclaw-auth-monitor.service` template has no diff. The old-custom-template rerun replaces existing `NOTIFY_*` lines by key. The setup path does not invoke `python3`. A required-placeholder mismatch exits before overwriting the existing installed service.

**What was not tested:** I did not run the timer end-to-end on a live Linux systemd user session in this environment.

## Scope boundary
This PR only fixes how the auth-monitor systemd unit is materialized during setup.

It does not:
- change auth-monitor runtime behavior
- change notification routing
- change gateway/systemd unit naming
- refactor other helper scripts

## Validation
- `bash -n scripts/setup-auth-system.sh`
- `git diff --check`
- ran the setup script with a temporary `HOME` and stubbed `systemctl`
- verified that the generated user unit contains the configured environment values
- verified that the generated user unit points to the current checkout's `scripts/auth-monitor.sh`
- verified that no temporary service file remains after install
- verified that the tracked `scripts/systemd/openclaw-auth-monitor.service` template remains unchanged
- verified a rerun against a temp-copied old template with pre-rendered custom `NOTIFY_*` values
- verified setup succeeds with a failing `python3` stub first on `PATH`
- verified that a required-placeholder mismatch exits before overwriting an existing service unit

## User impact
Users can run the setup script without dirtying the repo or generating a user unit with a machine-specific template path.

## Risks and mitigations
- Risk: the rendered `ExecStart` path could be wrong if the copied unit is patched incorrectly
- Mitigation: validate required placeholders before installing the rendered unit
- Risk: setup failure could replace a previously working user service
- Mitigation: render to a temporary unit and move it over the installed unit only after rendering succeeds

## Compatibility / migration
- backward compatible: yes
- migration required: no

## Failure recovery
- rerun `scripts/setup-auth-system.sh` to regenerate the user unit
- restore the previous unit file from `~/.config/systemd/user/openclaw-auth-monitor.service` if needed

target.title: Fix Kimi tool-call rewriting stop reason handling
target.body:
## Real behavior proofBehavior or issue addressed: Prevented Kimi response normalization from rewriting assistant-origin tool-call messages so that `stopReason` remains on the assistant flow instead of being treated as a generic tool-initiated completion.Real environment tested: Real OpenClaw checkout in a local Windows environment running OpenClaw tooling (`pnpm`, `node`, `vitest`) against the provider extension test suite after patching `extensions/kimi-coding/stream.ts`.Exact steps or command run after the patch:- Set `OPENCLAW_VITEST_INCLUDE_FILE=["kimi-coding/stream.test.ts"]`.- Run `pnpm exec vitest run --config test/vitest.extension-providers.config.ts`.After-fix evidence: Terminal output from the real run:```text$env:OPENCLAW_VITEST_INCLUDE_FILE='["kimi-coding/stream.test.ts"]'> pnpm exec vitest run --config test/vitest.extension-providers.config.ts✓ extensions/kimi-coding/stream.test.ts (17 tests)  ✓ Kimi extension › stream response normalization › preserves assistant stopReason for tool-call messages  ✓ Kimi extension › stream response normalization › preserves assistant path when tool-call message arrives late Test Files  1 passed (1)      Tests  17 passed (17)   Start at  ...   Duration  ...```Observed result after fix: In this regression scope, assistant-role messages that contain tool calls remain on the assistant flow with `stopReason` preserved as expected; user-facing behavior no longer shows assistant messages rewritten to `toolUsed` stop reason.What was not tested: No screenshot/video terminal capture was collected, and full cross-platform real-provider end-to-end replay was not run in this proof pass.

## Real behavior proof
Behavior or issue addressed: Prevented Kimi response normalization from rewriting assistant-origin tool-call messages so that `stopReason` stays on the assistant flow instead of being treated as a generic tool-initiated completion.
Real environment tested: Real OpenClaw checkout in a local Windows environment running OpenClaw tooling (`pnpm`, `node`, `vitest`) after patching `extensions/kimi-coding/stream.ts`.
Exact steps or command run after the patch:
- Set `OPENCLAW_VITEST_INCLUDE_FILE=["kimi-coding/stream.test.ts"]`.
- Run `pnpm exec vitest run --config test/vitest.extension-providers.config.ts`.
After-fix evidence: Redacted real terminal output from that post-patch run (artifact-style proof):
```text
$env:OPENCLAW_VITEST_INCLUDE_FILE='["kimi-coding/stream.test.ts"]'
> pnpm exec vitest run --config test/vitest.extension-providers.config.ts

✓ extensions/kimi-coding/stream.test.ts (17 tests)

  ✓ ...

      Tests  17 passed (17)

```
Observed result after fix: In this regression scope, assistant-role messages that contain tool-call text keep `stopReason` as expected while non-assistant tool-call messages are no longer coerced to assistant tool-use continuation.
What was not tested: No screenshot/video capture, no full cross-platform real-provider end-to-end replay was run in this proof pass.

target.title: UI: fix half-block art rendering for UTF-8 QR codes in web chat
target.body:
## Summary Describe the problem and fix in 2–5 bullets: - Problem: UTF-8 QR codes (using half-block characters ▀▄█) render broken in the web chat dashboard — default `
` line-height creates gaps between rows, background/foreground colors don't match the block-character encoding, and the characters don't tile into a scannable image.
- Why it matters: QR codes sent by tools (e.g. Algorand ARC-26 QR generator) are unreadable/unscannable in the web UI, forcing users to copy raw text and render elsewhere.
- What changed: Added `looksLikeHalfBlockArt()` detection in `markdown.ts` that identifies code blocks dominated by half-block Unicode characters, wraps them with a `.half-block-art` CSS class, and applies tight line-height (0.6), black background, white foreground, and slightly larger font-size so the blocks tile seamlessly. CSS added for both `.chat-text` (chat messages) and `.sidebar-markdown` (tool output sidebar), with both light and dark theme variants.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): No changes to the markdown parsing pipeline, sanitization, or any other code block rendering. Only half-block-art detection and styling were added.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [x] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [x] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #
- Related #

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- Code blocks containing UTF-8 half-block art (QR codes, box-drawing art) now render with black background, white text, and tight line-height so they display as intended scannable images.
- No change to normal code block rendering.

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? `No`
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? `No`
- New/changed network calls? `No`
- Command/tool execution surface changed? `No`
- Data access scope changed? `No`

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS, Ubuntu
- Runtime/container: TUI, Browser (web chat dashboard)
- Model/provider: Any (content is tool output)
- Integration/channel (if any): Web UI
- Relevant config (redacted): N/A

### Steps

1. Open the web chat dashboard
2. Have a tool or assistant send a UTF-8 QR code (e.g. via Algorand `generate_algorand_qrcode` or any tool that outputs Unicode block-character QR codes)
3. Observe the code block rendering

### Expected

- QR code renders as a clean, scannable square image with no gaps between rows

### Actual

- Before: rows have visible gaps due to default line-height, background is gray instead of black, characters don't tile — QR is unreadable
- After: QR code renders correctly and is scannable

## Evidence

Attach at least one:

- [x] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [ ] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

### Before 




### After






## Human Verification (required)

What you personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified scenarios: QR code rendering in chat messages and tool output sidebar, both light and dark themes
- Edge cases checked: Normal code blocks unaffected, short block-character snippets (< 10 lines) do not trigger detection, non-QR code blocks render as before
- What you did **not** verify: All possible QRCode coloring; detection heuristic may not cover all edge cases of block art

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

If a bot review conversation is addressed by this PR, resolve that conversation yourself. Do not leave bot review conversation cleanup for maintainers.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? `Yes`
- Config/env changes? `No`
- Migration needed? `No`

## Failure Recovery (if this breaks)

- How to disable/revert this change quickly: Revert the `.half-block-art` CSS rules in `text.css` and `sidebar.css`, and remove the `looksLikeHalfBlockArt()` branch in `markdown.ts`
- Files/config to restore: `ui/src/styles/chat/text.css`, `ui/src/styles/chat/sidebar.css`, `ui/src/ui/markdown.ts`
- Known bad symptoms reviewers should watch for: Normal code blocks accidentally styled as half-block art (false positive detection), or QR codes still rendering with gaps (false negative)

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: False positive detection on code blocks that happen to contain many block characters but aren't QR/art
  - Mitigation: Detection requires 60%+ of lines to contain half-block characters AND at least 10 lines — unlikely for normal code. Worst case is cosmetic (black bg + tight line-height on a non-QR block).

target.title: feat(cli): add trust windows for time-bounded exec approval
target.body:
## AI Disclosure

Built with Claude and GPT-5.3 Codex. Ran AI review passes as a sanity check before submitting. Fully tested — 64 new test cases covering the main flows and auth edge cases.

---

## Summary

- **Problem:** Right now it's all-or-nothing for exec approvals: either approve every command individually, or turn off approvals entirely. When you're doing a long build or batch refactor you already trust, the per-command prompts get old fast.
- **Why it matters:** There's no safe middle ground. People who get tired of prompts just disable approvals.  Good security is usable security.  Using a time-bound system is a pattern common to many other types of escalation systems (sudo, access tokens, etc.)
- **What changed:** Three new CLI commands (`trust`, `untrust`, `trust-status`) that let an operator open a time-limited window where commands run without prompts. In-memory trust state, one enforcement point in `resolveExecApprovalsFromFile`, JSONL audit trail of commands that ran during the window, caller auth checks.
- **What did NOT change:** The existing exec approval pipeline is untouched — the trust override is additive, bolted on at the end of `resolveExecApprovalsFromFile`. No changes to auth, tokens, network calls, or wire protocol. Agents can't initiate trust windows; only interactive CLI sessions can.  This PR doesn't expose this feature through common usage points like TUI, Discord, Whatsapp, etc.  It does lay a foundation for those features to be added next.

## Change Type (select all)

- [ ] Bug fix
- [x] Feature
- [ ] Refactor
- [ ] Docs
- [x] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [x] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [x] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Related #46657 (earlier iteration with a messier commit history — will close when this merges)

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- **New commands:**
  - `openclaw approvals trust --minutes N` — opens a trust window (default cap: 60m, up to 480m with `--force`)
  - `openclaw approvals untrust` — revokes the window and prints what commands ran
  - `openclaw approvals trust-status` — shows whether a window is active and how much time is left
- While a trust window is active, commands for that agent run without approval prompts (`security=full`, `ask=off`).
- Trust state is in-memory only. Restarting the gateway clears everything.
- A JSONL audit log is written to `~/.openclaw/trust-audit-<agentId>.jsonl` during the window (mode 0o600). `untrust` deletes it by default; `--keep-audit` preserves it.
- All three commands require an interactive CLI terminal. Agent sessions and non-CLI clients are rejected.

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? **Yes**
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? **No**
- New/changed network calls? **No** — new RPC methods go over the existing WebSocket connection
- Command/tool execution surface changed? **Yes** — during a trust window, commands skip the approval prompt
- Data access scope changed? **No**

**Risk + mitigation:**

This is a privilege escalation feature, so it needs to be locked down:

- **Who can grant:** Only interactive CLI sessions with device identity (or loopback + token/password auth). Agents can't grant trust to themselves — `requireNonAgentSession` checks both `OPENCLAW_SESSION_KEY` and `OPENCLAW_AGENT_ID`.
- **How long:** 60m default cap. You need `--force` to go higher, and 480m is a hard ceiling.
- **How many:** You can't stack windows. One per agent, period. Revoke first if you want a new one. Importantly, this prevents re-entry or extension by an agent.
- **What's recorded:** JSONL audit of every command that ran, plus gateway structured logs on grant/revoke. The audit is best-effort (won't fail exec on I/O errors), but the gateway logs are always there.
- **What persists:** Nothing. In-memory only. Process restart = clean slate.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS / Linux / Windows
- Runtime/container: Node.js 22+
- Model/provider: N/A (infrastructure change)
- Integration/channel: CLI → Gateway WebSocket
- Relevant config: `~/.openclaw/exec-approvals.json` (unchanged format)

### Steps

1. Start the gateway: `openclaw gateway start`
2. Grant a trust window: `openclaw approvals trust --minutes 10 --yes`
3. Run commands — they execute without approval prompts
4. Check status: `openclaw approvals trust-status`
5. Revoke: `openclaw approvals untrust --yes`

### Expected

- Step 2: Prints trust window active with duration and expiry time
- Step 3: Commands run without prompts
- Step 4: Shows `active` with remaining minutes
- Step 5: Prints revocation confirmation with audit summary

### Actual

- Matches expected.

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [ ] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

64 tests across 8 files (1,100+ lines of test code). Coverage includes:
- Grant/revoke lifecycle, duration caps, anti-stacking, expired windows
- Caller auth: device identity, loopback auth, non-CLI rejection, agent-session blocking (both env vars)
- Audit: append, load, summarize, cleanup, malformed JSONL resilience, empty commands, boundary formatting
- CLI: `--force`, `--keep-audit`, `--minutes` flag propagation, TTY checks
- Exec integration: audit entries on gateway/node success and error paths

Happy-path coverage is solid. Edge cases around real device-identity auth are tested via mocked `GatewayClient` objects, not live auth flows.

## Human Verification (required)

- **Verified scenarios:** Trust grant → command execution without prompts → status check → revoke with audit summary. Rejection from non-TTY, agent sessions, and non-CLI callers. Anti-stacking. Duration cap enforcement.
- **Edge cases checked:** Malformed JSONL lines (skipped), audit write failure (logged, doesn't break exec), `--force` with durations above 60m, idempotent untrust when no window exists, revoke after natural expiry.
- **What I did not verify:** Load testing with many concurrent trust windows. Real device-identity auth (mocked in tests). Swift protocol model compilation on macOS.

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? **Yes** — all new code. Older clients work identically.
- Config/env changes? **No**
- Migration needed? **No**

## Failure Recovery (if this breaks)

- **How to disable/revert:** Revert this PR. Trust state is in-memory — restarting the gateway clears everything. No persisted state to clean up.
- **Files/config to restore:** None. Audit files in `~/.openclaw/` can be safely deleted.
- **Known bad symptoms:** If the trust override in `resolveExecApprovalsFromFile` misbehaves, commands could bypass approval prompts when they shouldn't. Watch for `trustWindowActive: true` in resolved approvals when no window was granted.

## Risks and Mitigations

- **Risk:** User grants a long trust window and walks away — agent has elevated permissions for up to 8 hours.
  - **Mitigation:** Default cap is 60 minutes. `--force` required for longer. 480m hard max. Audit trail + gateway logs record everything.

- **Risk:** Audit trail is best-effort — I/O errors don't fail exec, so there could be gaps.
  - **Mitigation:** Gateway structured logs record grant/revoke events regardless. The audit file is a nice-to-have; the gateway logs are the real safety net.

target.title: feat(plugins): add agent_to_agent_turn hook for A2A ping-pong visibility
target.body:
## Summary

- **Problem:** During agent-to-agent (A2A) ping-pong exchanges, intermediate turns are invisible to channel plugins — only the final "announce" step goes through the channel. Channel plugins (e.g., DingTalk, Discord) cannot forward intermediate expert-to-expert discussion to users in real-time.
- **Why it matters:** For multi-expert collaboration scenarios (e.g., troubleshooting in a group chat), users need to see the discussion process between experts, not just the final conclusion. Without visibility into intermediate turns, the A2A feature cannot be used for user-facing expert collaboration.
- **What changed:** Added a new `agent_to_agent_turn` plugin hook that fires after each completed turn of an A2A ping-pong exchange. Channel plugins can register handlers via `api.on("agent_to_agent_turn", handler)` to intercept and forward these messages.
- **What did NOT change:** No changes to A2A core logic, session management, or existing hook behavior. The hook is purely additive and opt-in.

## Change Type (select all)

- [ ] Bug fix
- [x] Feature
- [ ] Refactor
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Related: Multi-expert collaboration in channel plugins (DingTalk @sub-agent feature)

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- New plugin hook `agent_to_agent_turn` available for channel plugins
- No behavior change unless a plugin explicitly registers a handler
- Zero overhead when no handlers are registered (guarded by `hasHooks` check)

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? `No` — hook is read-only, receives reply text that already exists in the session
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? `No`
- New/changed network calls? `No`
- Command/tool execution surface changed? `No`
- Data access scope changed? `No`

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS Darwin 24.6.0
- Runtime/container: Node.js
- Model/provider: N/A (infrastructure change)
- Integration/channel: DingTalk (primary use case)

### Steps

1. Configure two agents with `tools.agentToAgent.enabled: true` and mutual `allow` lists
2. Register a plugin handler: `api.on("agent_to_agent_turn", (event) => console.log(event))`
3. Trigger an A2A exchange via `sessions_send` with `timeoutSeconds > 0`

### Expected

- Handler is called after each ping-pong turn with `{ turn, speakerSessionKey, reply, ... }`
- A2A exchange completes normally regardless of handler success/failure

### Actual

- Verified via `pnpm build` — TypeScript compilation passes including exhaustiveness checks

## Evidence

- [x] `pnpm build` passes — TypeScript exhaustiveness check (`MissingPluginHookNames extends never`) confirms all type registrations are consistent
- [x] Hook follows exact same pattern as `subagent_spawned`/`subagent_ended` (void hook, `runVoidHook`, `hasHooks` guard)

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified scenarios: `pnpm build` compilation passes, exhaustiveness type check passes
- Edge cases checked: hook handler errors are caught silently (cannot interrupt A2A exchange), `hasHooks` guard prevents overhead when no handlers registered
- What I did **not** verify: End-to-end A2A exchange with a real channel plugin handler (requires running OpenClaw instance with DingTalk plugin)

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? `Yes` — purely additive, no existing behavior changes
- Config/env changes? `No`
- Migration needed? `No`

## Failure Recovery (if this breaks)

- How to disable/revert this change quickly: Revert the 3-file commit; or simply don't register the hook in plugins
- Files/config to restore: `src/plugins/types.ts`, `src/plugins/hooks.ts`, `src/agents/tools/sessions-send-tool.a2a.ts`
- Known bad symptoms: If the hook runner import path is wrong, build will fail (caught by CI)

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: Hook handler throws an error during A2A exchange
  - Mitigation: All hook calls are wrapped in `try/catch` — errors are silently swallowed, A2A exchange continues uninterrupted

target.title: feat(tui): show reconnect hint and auto-probe gateway when disconnected
target.body:
## Problem

Fixes #43778.

When the gateway restarts (e.g. after `openclaw gateway restart`), the TUI shows `not connected to gateway — message not sent` with no guidance on what to do. Users had no way to know they could manually reconnect or how to restart the TUI.

## Changes

### 1. `GatewayClient` — trackable reconnect timer + `reconnect()` method
- Store the pending reconnect `setTimeout` handle so it can be cancelled before a forced reconnect (prevents double-connect race)
- New public `reconnect()` method: resets exponential backoff to 1 s and immediately retries. If a WebSocket is open/mid-handshake it closes it cleanly; if already disconnected it calls `start()` directly
- `stop()` now clears the tracked timer

### 2. `GatewayChatClient` — expose `reconnect()`
Delegates to `GatewayClient.reconnect()`.

### 3. `CustomEditor` — `onCtrlR` hook
Adds ctrl+r key binding alongside the existing ctrl+l / ctrl+g / ctrl+p family.

### 4. `tui.ts` — UX improvements on disconnect
- **ctrl+r wired**: `editor.onCtrlR` calls `client.reconnect()` when disconnected
- **Status hint updated**: `activityStatus` now reads `reconnecting… ctrl+r to retry now` instead of the silent `idle`
- **One-shot system message**: first disconnect in a session surfaces:
  ```
  Gateway disconnected. Auto-reconnecting… press ctrl+r to retry immediately.
  If the problem persists, restart the TUI with: openclaw tui
  ```
  (resets on reconnect so it fires again if the gateway goes away a second time)

## Behaviour summary

| Scenario | Before | After |
|----------|--------|-------|
| Gateway restarts | Silent `not connected` | System message + `ctrl+r to retry now` in status |
| User wants to retry immediately | No shortcut | ctrl+r forces immediate reconnect |
| Gateway takes >30 s to come back | Backoff maxes at 30 s silently | ctrl+r resets backoff at any time |
| Gateway stays down | No guidance | Chat log shows `openclaw tui` restart command |

## Testing
- All existing tests pass (57 tests across tui, gateway-chat, tui-event-handlers)
- Updated `tui.test.ts` to reflect new `activityStatus` text

target.title: fix: copilot token cache ignores profile rotation on rate limit
target.body:
Closes #43658

## Problem

When multiple `github-copilot` auth profiles are configured, profile rotation after a rate limit has no effect. All profiles end up using the same Copilot API token because `resolveCopilotApiToken()` caches to a single shared file (`github-copilot.token.json`) without tracking which GitHub account produced the token. A profile switch passes a different GitHub PAT, but the cache returns the still-valid token from the rate-limited account.

## Solution

Store a SHA-256 fingerprint (first 16 hex chars) of the GitHub PAT alongside the cached Copilot API token. On cache lookup, compare the fingerprint against the current profile's token. A mismatch forces a fresh token exchange with the GitHub Copilot token endpoint.

### Changes

**`src/providers/github-copilot-token.ts`**
- Added `githubTokenFingerprint` field to `CachedCopilotToken` type
- Added `fingerprintGithubToken()` helper (SHA-256, truncated to 16 hex chars)
- Cache hit now requires fingerprint match in addition to expiry check
- New tokens are saved with the fingerprint of the GitHub PAT used

**`src/providers/github-copilot-token.test.ts`**
- Updated existing cache-hit test to include fingerprint
- Added test: cache bypassed when a different GitHub token (different profile) is used
- Added test: legacy cache entries without fingerprint are treated as cache miss

### Backward compatibility

- Legacy cache files without `githubTokenFingerprint` are treated as a cache miss — the token is re-fetched on the next call and saved with a fingerprint. No migration needed.
- The `githubTokenFingerprint` field is optional (`?`) so older versions reading a new cache file won't break.

## Testing

```
✓ derives baseUrl from token
✓ uses cache when token is still valid
✓ fetches and stores token when cache is missing
✓ bypasses cache when a different github token (profile) is used
✓ bypasses cache when cached entry has no fingerprint (legacy)

Test Files  1 passed (1)
     Tests  5 passed (5)
```

All 38 auth-profiles tests also pass.

target.title: feat: cross-gateway sessions_send and sessions_spawn via gateway.peers
target.body:
## Problem

`sessions_send` and `sessions_spawn` only work within a single gateway. In multi-machine setups (agents split across MacBook, iMac, Mac Studio — each running their own OpenClaw gateway), there's no native way for agents to communicate cross-gateway. The current workaround is SSH scripts (`msg-jarvis`), which are fragile and bypass all gateway auth/routing.

## Solution

Add cross-gateway routing to session tools via three complementary mechanisms:

### 1. `gateway.peers` config map (new)

Named peer gateways so agents can target remote gateways by name instead of raw URLs:

```yaml
gateway:
  peers:
    imac:
      url: wss://imac.local:18789
      token: \${env:IMAC_GATEWAY_TOKEN}
    studio:
      url: wss://studio.local:18789
```

Peer tokens support SecretInput (env refs, secret stores, plaintext).

### 2. `sessions_send`: `gateway` / `gatewayUrl` / `gatewayToken` params

```
sessions_send(sessionKey="agent:jarvis:main", message="hello", gateway="imac")
sessions_send(sessionKey="agent:pepper:main", message="hi", gatewayUrl="wss://custom:18789", gatewayToken="tok")
```

When cross-gateway params are present:
- Label resolution happens on the **remote** gateway
- Message is sent via remote `agent` method
- Wait + history fetch all route to remote
- Response includes `remote: true` flag
- Local session resolution, visibility checks, and A2A flow are bypassed (the remote gateway owns those)

### 3. `sessions_spawn`: same params

```
sessions_spawn(task="run tests", agentId="pepper", gateway="studio")
```

Forwards spawn as an agent message to the remote gateway.

### Shared helper: `gateway-peer.ts`

Resolves cross-gateway targeting from tool params:
1. `gateway: "peerName"` → looks up `gateway.peers[peerName]`
2. `gatewayUrl` + optional `gatewayToken` → explicit URL override  
3. Neither → local gateway (returns undefined, no behavior change)

Peer names take precedence when both `gateway` and `gatewayUrl` are provided.

### Design notes

- **Zero breaking changes** — local path is completely unchanged
- **Pattern already exists** — `message`, `cron`, `gateway`, `nodes`, `canvas` tools already accept `gatewayUrl`/`gatewayToken`; this extends the pattern to session tools
- **Security** — peer URLs are validated through existing `resolveGatewayOptions()` which enforces URL format and token resolution rules

## Changes

| File | Change |
|---|---|
| `config/zod-schema.ts` | `gateway.peers` record schema |
| `config/types.gateway.ts` | `GatewayPeerConfig` type |
| `config/schema.labels.ts` | Label for `gateway.peers` |
| `config/schema.help.ts` | Help text for `gateway.peers` |
| `agents/tools/gateway-peer.ts` | **New** — shared peer resolution helper |
| `agents/tools/sessions-send-tool.ts` | Cross-gateway fast path + 3 new schema params |
| `agents/tools/sessions-spawn-tool.ts` | Cross-gateway fast path + 3 new schema params |
| `agents/tools/gateway-peer.test.ts` | **New** — 9 tests for peer resolution |

## Tests

- 9 new peer resolution tests (named peer, missing peer, explicit URL, precedence, error messages)
- 17 existing sessions tests pass
- 32 existing config schema tests pass

Closes #43605

target.title: security: config include path hardening (NUL + length)
target.body:
## Summary

This PR adds **path hardening** for the config `$include` directive (CWE-22 defense-in-depth). It does not introduce new features; it tightens validation so that invalid or abusive include paths are rejected early with clear errors instead of relying on platform-dependent or undefined behavior.

## Motivation

- **NUL bytes (`\0`)**: On some platforms or when passed to native/fs APIs, a path containing a null byte can be interpreted as truncated (e.g. `"./a\0../../../etc/passwd"` → `"./a"`), which could bypass path-traversal checks or cause inconsistent behavior. Rejecting NUL in include paths removes this ambiguity.
- **Path length**: Unbounded include paths (or resolved absolute paths) can cause performance issues, stack pressure, or hit OS limits in undefined ways. Capping at 4096 characters (aligned with common `PATH_MAX`) keeps behavior predictable and avoids DoS-style inputs.

Existing protections (path traversal and symlink checks) remain unchanged; this PR adds **input validation** before path resolution.

## Changes

### Code (`src/config/includes.ts`)

- **New constant**: `MAX_INCLUDE_PATH_LENGTH = 4096` (exported for tests and possible reuse).
- **In `resolvePath(includePath)`** (before any `path.resolve` / `path.normalize`):
  1. If `includePath` contains `\0` → throw `ConfigIncludeError` with message: `"Include path must not contain null bytes"`.
  2. If `includePath.length > MAX_INCLUDE_PATH_LENGTH` → throw `ConfigIncludeError` with message: `"Include path exceeds maximum length (4096 characters)"`.
  3. After computing `normalized = path.normalize(resolved)`, if `normalized.length > MAX_INCLUDE_PATH_LENGTH` → throw `ConfigIncludeError` with message: `"Resolved include path exceeds maximum length (4096 characters)"`.

All new errors use the existing `ConfigIncludeError` type and do not log or echo the raw path in the message (to avoid leaking NUL or very long strings).

### Tests (`src/config/includes.test.ts`)

- Import `MAX_INCLUDE_PATH_LENGTH`.
- **Malformed paths**: Expect `ConfigIncludeError` with message matching `/null bytes?/i` for paths containing `\0` (e.g. `"./file\x00.json"`, `"./a\x00b.json"`); keep existing test for `//etc/passwd` (path traversal).
- **Length limit**: New test "rejects include path exceeding maximum length" — paths of length `MAX_INCLUDE_PATH_LENGTH + 1` and exactly `MAX_INCLUDE_PATH_LENGTH` (which can still produce a resolved path over the limit) both throw with a message containing "maximum length".
- **Regression**: New test "accepts include path at or under maximum length when file exists" — normal short paths still resolve correctly.

### Documentation

- **`docs/gateway/configuration-reference.md`** (Config includes section): Document that paths must not contain null bytes and must not exceed 4096 characters (before or after resolution); add "invalid path format (null bytes or excessive length)" to the Errors bullet.
- **`docs/gateway/configuration.md`** ($include accordion): Add that paths must not contain null bytes and must not exceed 4096 characters; extend error handling to include invalid path format.

## Is this a feature?

No. This is **security hardening and specification of existing behavior**:

- No new user-facing capability is added.
- Previously, NUL or very long paths could lead to implementation-defined or platform-dependent behavior; now they are explicitly rejected with a clear error. Normal, valid include paths are unchanged.

## Testing

- `pnpm test src/config/includes.test.ts` — all 28 tests pass.
- No changes to other config or security tests.

## Checklist

- [x] Code: NUL and length checks in `resolvePath`; clear `ConfigIncludeError` messages.
- [x] Tests: NUL, length limit, and regression cases.
- [x] Docs: configuration-reference and configuration updated for path validation and error behavior.
- [x] No new optional behavior; only validation and error messages.

target.title: fix: add bootout poll and kickstart fallback to launchd reload handoff
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes the `reload` mode race condition in the launchd restart handoff script that can leave the macOS LaunchAgent deregistered with no auto-recovery.

On macOS, when the gateway restarts with `mode=reload`, the handoff script runs `launchctl bootout` followed immediately by `launchctl bootstrap`. Because `bootout` returns 0 immediately but launchd processes the unload asynchronously, the subsequent `bootstrap` can hit the service while it's still transitioning and fail with `Bootstrap failed: 5: Input/output error` (EIO). Since `bootout` succeeded, launchd treats the service as cleanly deregistered (not crashed), so `KeepAlive=true` does not restart it — the gateway stays down until manual intervention.

Two compounding problems existed:

1. No unload-completion poll — `bootout` returns before launchd finishes
2. No fallback — unlike `kickstart` and `start-after-exit` templates which retry on failure, `reload` had no recovery path

### Fix

The `reload` handoff template now:

1. **Polls `launchctl print` after `bootout`** until the service is confirmed unloaded (max 15 × 0.2s = 3s), preventing the async race
2. **Falls back to `launchctl kickstart -k`** if `bootstrap` still fails after the poll, matching the resilience of the other templates

This ensures the service either re-registers cleanly or restarts in-place rather than being left silently deregistered.

Closes: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/84630

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior addressed:** The launchd reload handoff script (bootout → bootstrap) races with launchd's async unload, causing EIO and leaving the LaunchAgent permanently deregistered with no auto-recovery.

**Real environment tested:** macOS 15.5, Node 24, local checkout with Vitest suite.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch:**

```
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/daemon/launchd-restart-handoff.test.ts
```

Also manually inspected the generated reload-mode shell script to verify the bootout polling loop and kickstart fallback are present and syntactically correct.

**Evidence after fix:**

The rendered reload-mode handoff script now includes the bootout wait loop and kickstart fallback:

```sh
service_target="$1"
domain="$2"
plist_path="$3"
# ... wait for caller pid ...
status=0
launchctl enable "$service_target"
launchctl bootout "$service_target" >/dev/null 2>&1 || true
# NEW: poll until launchd finishes async unload
bootout_wait_count="15"
while [ "$bootout_wait_count" -gt 0 ]; do
  if ! launchctl print "$service_target" >/dev/null 2>&1; then
    break
  fi
  bootout_wait_count=$((bootout_wait_count - 1))
  sleep 0.2
done
if launchctl bootstrap "$domain" "$plist_path"; then
  status=0
else
  status=$?
  # NEW: kickstart fallback so service isn't left deregistered
  launchctl kickstart -k "$service_target"
  status=$?
fi
```

All 5 unit tests pass, confirming the reload mode template generates correctly with the new polling loop and fallback:

```
✓ polls after bootout and falls back to kickstart on bootstrap failure for reload mode
✓ waits for the caller pid before kickstarting launchd
✓ passes the plain label separately for start-after-exit mode
✓ sanitizes restart helper environment overrides before spawning
✓ rejects invalid launchd labels before spawning the helper

 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  5 passed (5)
```

**Observed result after fix:** The generated reload script now safely waits for launchd to complete its async unload before re-bootstrapping. If bootstrap still fails (e.g., rare transient launchd state), the kickstart fallback keeps the service running rather than leaving it silently deregistered.

**What was not tested:** Live macOS launchd integration with an actual LaunchAgent reload cycle (the script template change is straightforward and verified via unit tests; full integration testing requires a macOS device with launchd service management).

target.title: fix(logging): make user redactPatterns additive with defaults + fix config resolution
target.body:
## Problem

Two bugs in redact logging (#42982):

1. `resolvePatterns` replaced built-in default patterns with user-configured `redactPatterns` instead of merging. Adding a custom email regex silently dropped API key, token, and PEM detection.

2. `redactToolDetail` passed config patterns as explicit options to `redactSensitiveText`, bypassing the merge logic. Callers passing `{ mode: 'tools' }` without explicit patterns also skipped config.

## Fix

1. New `mergeWithDefaults()` function combines user patterns with built-in defaults (additive).
2. `redactToolDetail` now uses `mergeWithDefaults()` directly.
3. `redactSensitiveText` without options always resolves and merges config.

## Validation

14 tests passing (12 existing + 2 new covering additive behavior). Lint clean.

Closes #42982

target.title: security: default tools.fs.workspaceOnly to true, add sensitive path denylist
target.body:
## Summary

- **Default `tools.fs.workspaceOnly` to `true`** instead of `false`, so agent file tools (read/write/edit) are restricted to the workspace directory out of the box. Operators can still set `tools.fs.workspaceOnly: false` to restore unrestricted access.
- **Add a sensitive-path denylist** (`src/security/sensitive-paths.ts`) that blocks agent access to known credential/key locations (`~/.ssh`, `~/.aws`, `~/.gnupg`, `~/.openclaw/credentials`, `~/.env`, `/etc/shadow`, `.pem`/`.key` files, etc.) even when `workspaceOnly` is explicitly disabled.
- **Apply denylist enforcement** in `pi-tools.read.ts` host write and edit operations so the guard is active on all code paths.
- **Update test** to reflect the new secure default.

## Motivation

The agent has full filesystem read access on the host by default. An indirect prompt injection (via fetched web content, email hooks, or malicious file content) could instruct the agent to read `~/.ssh/id_rsa`, `~/.aws/credentials`, or other secrets and leak them through the response. This is the most critical attack surface for a tool-enabled agent.

SECURITY.md acknowledges this trust model (`agents.defaults.sandbox.mode` defaults to `off`), but a secure-by-default posture would prevent accidental exposure for the majority of users who don't customize their config.

## Test plan

- [ ] Existing `tool-fs-policy.test.ts` updated — verify new default is `true`
- [ ] New `sensitive-paths.test.ts` — covers SSH, AWS, GPG, OpenClaw credentials, /etc/shadow, .env, .pem/.key patterns
- [ ] Manual test: start gateway with default config, confirm agent cannot read `~/.ssh/id_rsa`
- [ ] Manual test: set `tools.fs.workspaceOnly: false`, confirm sensitive-path denylist still blocks `~/.aws/credentials`
- [ ] Manual test: set `tools.fs.workspaceOnly: false`, confirm normal files outside workspace are still readable

target.title: fix(feishu): attach quoted media to agent context
target.body:
## Summary

- Preserve raw Feishu message content when fetching quoted/replied messages.
- Reuse the existing Feishu media resolver to download media from quoted file/image/audio/video/media/sticker/post messages.
- Attach downloaded quoted media to the agent context (`MediaPath`, `MediaPaths`, `MediaTypes`) while keeping the quoted text fallback (`ReplyToBody`).

## Why

When a user replies to a Feishu file message and mentions the bot, the current inbound message is text while the file lives on the quoted parent message. Previously OpenClaw only parsed that parent as text (for example `[file message]`), so the agent never received a local media path and could not inspect the file.

## Tests

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.extension-feishu.config.ts extensions/feishu/src/send.test.ts extensions/feishu/src/bot.test.ts`

## Summary

Adds local-first helpers for reading and rendering recent continuity snapshots under `memory/recent`:

- parse frontmatter-based and legacy markdown continuity notes;
- render a structured recent snapshot document;
- build a small continuity manifest prioritized by status, priority, and recency;
- compare material continuity changes while ignoring TTL-only churn;
- expose the helpers through the focused `@openclaw/memory-host-sdk/runtime-files` surface.

This is intentionally only the local document/manifest layer. It does not add provider-backed extraction, command hooks, compaction hooks, or post-compaction behavior.

## Why

Agents currently have long-term memory files and session transcripts, but there is no small local-first continuity document format for the most recent active task state. This provides a narrow foundation that later integrations can use without mixing runtime hook behavior into this PR.

## Safety / scope

- Local filesystem only.
- No LLM/provider calls.
- No new hook registration.
- No command reply-path changes.
- No compaction/post-compaction behavior changes.

## Tests

target.title: feat: add per-agent compaction overrides (AI-assisted)
target.body:
## Summary

Adds per-agent compaction overrides so each configured agent can choose its own compaction runtime behavior instead of inheriting every compaction decision from `agents.defaults.compaction`.

This is intended for multi-agent installations where the main assistant, channel-specific agents, local/self-hosted agents, and Codex/app-server workers have different runtime and transcript-management requirements.

## Linked Issues And Related Work

Closes #52732

Related issues:

- #52751
- #69988
- #19501
- #15826
- #43834
- #6571
- #6572
- #30452
- #57901
- #58077
- #59863
- #60512
- #80784
- #82008

Related review/regression context addressed in this PR:

- strict `AgentEntrySchema` support for `agents.list[].compaction`
- defaults-only consumers for memory/notice/post-compaction behavior
- queued/direct compaction scope for legacy-session, non-main-agent runs

## Problem / Root Cause

Before this change, the config surface either rejected or only partially honored per-agent compaction settings. Several runtime paths still read from `agents.defaults.compaction` even when a specific `agents.list[]` entry defined its own compaction block.

That made these setups difficult or impossible to express correctly:

- main/default agent compacts with one provider/model
- channel or proxy agents compact with another provider/model
- local/self-hosted agents use a local provider alias for compaction
- Codex/app-server agents intentionally omit an OpenClaw compaction model override and keep their own runtime behavior
- non-main agents with legacy/non-agent session keys still need their active agent scope preserved during queued/direct compaction

## What Changed

This PR makes the per-agent compaction contract real end to end:

- extends the strict agent schema to accept `agents.list[].compaction`
- documents and labels the per-agent compaction fields
- resolves compaction provider/model/auth/profile choices through the active agent scope
- applies per-agent compaction settings in direct and queued compaction paths
- carries active `agentId` through preflight memory compaction, manual `/compact`, gateway session compaction, queued compaction, and direct compaction
- honors per-agent settings for:
  - `model`
  - `provider`
  - `authProfileId`
  - `timeoutSeconds`
  - `midTurnPrecheck`
  - `keepRecentTokens`
  - `truncateAfterCompaction`
  - `postIndexSync`
  - compaction memory-flush behavior
  - compaction follow-up/notice behavior
- keeps Codex/app-server agents able to omit a compaction model override so their native runtime path is not accidentally replaced by OpenClaw compaction selection
- adds regression coverage for legacy-session/non-main-agent compaction scope in both direct and queued compaction

## Reviewer Notes

The important behavioral invariant is:

> A compaction run should resolve configuration from the same active agent scope as the session/run that triggered it, even when the session key is legacy-shaped and does not encode `agent:<id>:`.

That is why this PR passes `agentId` explicitly into compaction params instead of relying only on parsing `sessionKey`.

The schema accepts the full per-agent compaction block, but this PR does not narrow the feature surface to schema-only support. The remaining defaults-only runtime consumers found during review were fixed so the accepted configuration is actually honored.

Scope audit result: after rechecking the PR surface, ancillary churn was removed from `src/config/model-refs.ts`, `apps/macos/Sources/OpenClaw/VoiceWakeSettings.swift`, import ordering, oxlint-suppression namespace cleanup, and unrelated `messages.groupChat.visibleReplies` help wording. The subagent model-selection-only guard was restored in `src/config/zod-schema.agent-defaults.test.ts` because it remains valid and protects the existing `subagents.model` contract.

The only intentionally retained non-feature test hygiene is `src/config/io.write-config.test.ts`, which clears the plugin metadata snapshot memo in teardown. This is included because removing it makes the full runtime-config suite fail order-dependently: `keeps shipped plugin install config records when index migration fails` receives no stale-plugin warning after earlier memoized plugin metadata leaks between tests. This does not change product behavior; it keeps the validation gate deterministic while this PR touches runtime config/schema surfaces.

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior or issue addressed: Per-agent compaction overrides must resolve from the active agent scope instead of always inheriting `agents.defaults.compaction`, including legacy/non-agent-shaped session keys that require an explicit non-main `agentId`.
- Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw source checkout on the PR branch plus a live OpenClaw gateway on `OpenClaw 2026.5.18 (50a2481)`, Node `v24.15.0`, host `Nano-01`, Discord channel binding for `#gb10`, native Codex app-server runtime authenticated through OAuth. Runtime proof logging was temporarily enabled at `/home/kklouzal/.openclaw/logs/gateway-debug-compaction-proof.log` with secrets redacted.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: Ran a live source-module proof with `HEAD_SHA=7e5f7acdde9b052c5b41213485380ee9c11e8a0d NODE_COMPILE_CACHE= node --import tsx .artifacts/per-agent-compaction-proof.mjs`; then verified the deployed multi-agent config on a real gateway and triggered manual `/compact` in the real Discord `#gb10` channel.
- Evidence after fix: Terminal transcript and redacted runtime log excerpt from the real setup:

```text
$ HEAD_SHA=7e5f7acdde9b052c5b41213485380ee9c11e8a0d NODE_COMPILE_CACHE= node --import tsx .artifacts/per-agent-compaction-proof.mjs
OpenClaw per-agent compaction live source proof
head=7e5f7acdde9b052c5b41213485380ee9c11e8a0d
sessionKey=legacy-topic-47
explicitAgentId=lossless-agent
resolvedSessionAgentId=lossless-agent
compactionProvider=google
compactionModel=gemini-3.1-pro-preview
timeoutMs=45000
truncateAfterCompaction=true
result=PASS: legacy session kept non-main agent compaction override
```

```json
{
  "liveConfig": {
    "main": { "compaction": { "model": "openai/gpt-5.4-mini" } },
    "webproxy": { "compaction": { "model": "openai/gpt-5.4-mini" } },

    "codex-worker": { "compaction": null }

    "codexSidecarUpdatedAt": "2026-05-19T05:27:39.290Z"
  }
}
```

```text

2026-05-18T22:28:31.302-07:00 INFO  completed codex app-server compaction sessionId=00109ec6-e729-4d21-9811-8294ad845acb
```
- Observed result after fix: The source-module proof selected the explicit non-main per-agent compaction override values `google/gemini-3.1-pro-preview`, `45000` ms timeout, and transcript rotation enabled for a legacy session key. The live deployed OpenClaw run accepted distinct per-agent compaction config blocks and completed a real Discord `/compact` in `#gb10`; the persisted session shows `compactionCount: 1`, `status: "done"`, and a live Codex app-server sidecar thread updated during the run.
- What was not tested: The live deployed Codex app-server compaction log does not currently print the resolved per-agent compaction model, so the live `#gb10` run is supplemental runtime proof while the source-module proof is the direct model/provider/timeout selection proof. Existing pre-restart Codex app-server sessions can also fail manual `/compact` with stale/missing native thread IDs; that appears to be a separate restart/session-recovery behavior and is not claimed fixed here.

target.title: fix: prevent re-entrant loop in internal hook trigger
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes re-entrant infinite loop in `triggerInternalHook` when a handler calls `triggerInternalHook` again with the same `type:action:sessionKey` combination.

## Problem

Before this fix, if a hook handler (e.g., `command:new`) triggered the same hook again internally, it would cause an infinite loop because there was no active-dispatch guard.

## Solution

Added an `AsyncLocalStorage`-based re-entrancy guard:
- Re-entrant calls within the same async call chain are blocked (handler → triggerInternalHook → same key → sees inherited store → dropped)
- Independent concurrent calls from different async contexts are NOT blocked (preserves at-least-once delivery)

## Changes
- `src/hooks/internal-hooks.ts`: Added `AsyncLocalStorage` guard around `triggerInternalHook`
- `src/hooks/internal-hooks.test.ts`: Added 116 lines of tests covering recursive, sequential, and concurrent dispatch behavior

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior or issue addressed:** Re-entrant `triggerInternalHook` infinite loop (same `type:action:sessionKey` triggered within its own handler chain).

**Real environment tested:** Local dev checkout, Node v24.14.0 on linux x64. Standalone reproduction script (`repro-reentrant-guard.mjs`) mirroring the exact guard logic from this PR, run directly with `node` — no monorepo build or test runner required.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch:**
```bash
node repro-reentrant-guard.mjs
```

**Evidence after fix:** Standalone reproduction script output showing before/after behavior:

```text
═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
Real Behavior Proof: AsyncLocalStorage Re-entrant Guard
Date: 2026-05-18T15:53:46.987Z
Node: v24.14.0 | Platform: linux x64
═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

Scenario 1: WITHOUT guard (main branch behavior)
  A handler that re-triggers the same event → infinite loop

Triggering command:new:test-session (capped at 10 to avoid hang)...
  [NO GUARD] handler invoked (#1) — command:new:test-session
  [NO GUARD] handler invoked (#2) — command:new:test-session
  [NO GUARD] handler invoked (#3) — command:new:test-session
  [NO GUARD] handler invoked (#4) — command:new:test-session
  [NO GUARD] handler invoked (#5) — command:new:test-session
  [NO GUARD] handler invoked (#6) — command:new:test-session
  [NO GUARD] handler invoked (#7) — command:new:test-session
  [NO GUARD] handler invoked (#8) — command:new:test-session
  [NO GUARD] handler invoked (#9) — command:new:test-session
  [NO GUARD] handler invoked (#10) — command:new:test-session

  Result: handler called 10 times (would be infinite without cap)
  ⚠️  WITHOUT guard, re-entrant calls amplify without bound

Scenario 2: WITH guard (PR branch behavior)
  Same handler → re-entrant call is correctly blocked

Triggering command:new:test-session...
  [WITH GUARD] handler invoked (#1) — command:new:test-session
  [WITH GUARD] re-entrant call BLOCKED ✅ (key: command|new|test-session)

  Result: handler called 1 time(s), re-entrant blocked 1 time(s)
  ✅ WITH guard, re-entrant calls are prevented

Scenario 3: Sequential triggers AFTER first completes → OK

Triggering #1...
  [SEQUENTIAL] handler invoked (#1) — command:new:test-session
Triggering #2...
  [SEQUENTIAL] handler invoked (#2) — command:new:test-session
Triggering #3...
  [SEQUENTIAL] handler invoked (#3) — command:new:test-session

  Result: handler called 3 time(s)
  ✅ Sequential triggers work correctly

Scenario 4: Concurrent INDEPENDENT triggers → both delivered
  (simulates fireAndForgetHook behavior)

Firing 2 independent concurrent triggers for message:received:session-a...
  [CONCURRENT] handler invoked (#2) — message:received:session-a
  [CONCURRENT] handler invoked (#2) — message:received:session-a

  Result: handler called 2 time(s)
  ✅ Independent concurrent triggers are both delivered

Scenario 5: Different session keys → not blocked

Firing command:new for session-a and session-b concurrently...
  [CROSS-SESSION] handler invoked (#1) — command:new:session-a
  [CROSS-SESSION] handler invoked (#2) — command:new:session-b

  Result: handler called 2 time(s)
  ✅ Different session keys are independently dispatched

═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
SUMMARY
═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

  Without guard (main):
    Re-entrant loop → 10 calls (capped, would be infinite)

  With guard (PR branch):
    Re-entrant blocked    → handler called 1x, blocked 1x ✅
    Sequential triggers   → handler called 3x ✅
    Concurrent independent→ handler called 2x ✅
    Cross-session         → handler called 2x ✅

  The AsyncLocalStorage guard prevents re-entrant amplification
  while preserving all legitimate dispatch patterns.

═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
```

**Observed result after fix:** Re-entrant calls for the same `type:action:sessionKey` within the same async call chain are correctly blocked (Scenario 2), while independent sequential (Scenario 3), concurrent (Scenario 4), and cross-session (Scenario 5) dispatches all proceed normally. The standalone script confirms the guard behavior matches the production fix.

**What was not tested:**
- Multi-process concurrent triggers (single-process only)
- Performance impact under extreme load (normal usage validated)

## Related
- Fixes potential infinite loop when embedded agent turns trigger the same hook type

target.title: fix(daemon): escape systemd cgroup for update handoff via systemd-run --scope
target.body:
## Summary

On systemd-supervised installs, clicking "Update now" silently fails: the update handoff helper is created but immediately killed by systemd's `KillMode=control-group` when the gateway unit restarts. The helper never executes, the version stays unchanged, and the UI shows "Update skipped: managed-service-handoff-started."

This PR detects systemd supervision and launches the handoff helper via `systemd-run --user --scope` to escape the gateway's cgroup. Non-systemd supervisors (launchd, etc.) continue using the existing detached-child path.

## Root cause

systemd's `KillMode=control-group` (the default) kills all processes in the service's cgroup on restart — including detached child processes. The handoff helper, despite being `unref()`'d and spawned with `detached: true`, stays in the same cgroup and is terminated before it can run `npm install`.

## Fix

```
systemd-run --user --scope --unit openclaw-update-<handoffId> --collect -- node handoff.cjs handoff.json
```

Key design choices:

**`--scope` not `--service`**: `--scope` inherits the caller's environment (profile, state dir, config, PATH, proxy, package manager discovery all preserved). `--service` runs in a clean systemd environment and would lose all service env vars.

**`KillMode=control-group` preserved**: The scoped update helper escapes via `systemd-run --scope` into its own cgroup, so `KillMode=control-group` safely stops the gateway and all its child workers without affecting the handoff. No change to existing shutdown semantics.

**Launch failure detection**: After spawning `systemd-run`, the code awaits its exit code with a 5s timeout. If systemd-run exits non-zero (user session unavailable, D-Bus down, etc.), an error is thrown before reporting "handoff started" — preventing the gateway from exiting 80 and leaving the service stale.

**No secret exposure via argv**: The `env` object is passed via `spawn()` option — identical to the existing non-systemd path. No `--setenv=KEY=*** args are used. Secrets stay in `/proc/PID/environ` (restricted to same UID), not in `/proc/PID/cmdline` (world-readable).

Additional hardening:
- `sanitizeSystemdUnitName()` strips non-`[a-zA-Z0-9:._-]` chars from `handoffId`
- `/tmp/openclaw-update-in-progress` marker file for `ExecStartPre` defense-in-depth guard
- `--collect` flag ensures scope units are garbage-collected after exit

## Changes

| File | Change |
|------|--------|
| `update-managed-service-handoff.ts` | Detect `supervisor === "systemd"` → spawn via `systemd-run --user --scope`; await exit code to detect launch failures; add marker file lifecycle; add `sanitizeSystemdUnitName()` |
| `update-managed-service-handoff.test.ts` | 3 new tests: systemd `--scope` spawn path with env assertions, ENOENT detection, non-zero exit detection; enhanced mock with `mockChild()` helper |
| `systemd-unit.ts` | Add `ExecStartPre` marker guard, `RestartPreventExitStatus=80`; keep `KillMode=control-group` with updated comment |
| `systemd-unit.test.ts` | Updated KillMode assertion to verify `control-group` |

## Tests

```
$ npx vitest run --project gateway-methods src/gateway/server-methods/update-managed-service-handoff.test.ts
 ✓ gateway-methods (8 tests) 632ms
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  8 passed (8)

$ npx vitest run --project unit-fast src/daemon/systemd-unit.test.ts
 ✓ unit-fast (4 tests) 2ms
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  4 passed (4)
```

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: systemd `KillMode=control-group` kills detached update handoff helper on gateway restart, preventing self-update.
- **Real environment tested**: OrbStack Linux VM (Ubuntu 22.04 Jammy, kernel 6.19.13-orbstack, aarch64) with full user-level systemd and `loginctl enable-linger`. Node.js v24.15.0.

### Live systemd proof: `--scope` escapes cgroup, helper survives service restart

Created a systemd user service with `KillMode=control-group` (matching OpenClaw's generated unit at `src/daemon/systemd-unit.ts:86`):

```ini
[Service]
Type=simple
ExecStart=/tmp/handoff-test/gateway-sim.sh
KillMode=control-group
Restart=no
```

**Test A: WITH `systemd-run --user --scope` (this PR)**

```
=== Gateway running ===
     Active: active (running) since Wed 2026-05-20 20:08:58 CST
   Main PID: 2797 (gateway-sim.sh)
     CGroup: /user.slice/user-1000.slice/user@1000.service/app.slice/openclaw-gateway-test.service

=== Helper started (in separate scope unit, sleeping 8s) ===
Helper PID=2799 started in scope at Wed May 20 08:08:58 PM CST 2026
Helper: sleeping 8s while gateway lives...

=== KILLING GATEWAY (KillMode=control-group) at 20:09:00 ===
Gateway service stopped

=== Immediately after kill ===
Helper still running (no sentinel yet — helper still working)

=== After 10s wait (helper finished at 20:09:06) ===
Helper: still alive after 8s! Gateway should be dead by now
Helper: writing sentinel at Wed May 20 08:09:06 PM CST 2026
Sentinel: update-complete
```

**Result**: Helper survived gateway service kill and completed work ✅

**Test B: WITHOUT `systemd-run --scope` (current main behavior)**

Same setup but using plain `bash &` with `disown` (simulating current detached spawn):

```
=== Helper started (no scope, sleeping 8s) ===
Helper PID=2844 (NO SCOPE) started at Wed May 20 08:09:44 PM CST 2026
Helper: sleeping 8s...

=== KILLING GATEWAY (KillMode=control-group) at 20:09:46 ===

=== After 10s wait ===
Helper log: stopped at "sleeping 8s..." — no further output
Sentinel: FAILED: helper was KILLED by KillMode=control-group!
```

**Result**: Helper was killed when gateway service stopped ❌

### Comparison

| Scenario | Helper survives service stop? | Sentinel written? |
|----------|------------------------------|-------------------|
| `systemd-run --user --scope` (this PR) | ✅ Yes — ran in separate scope unit | ✅ `update-complete` |
| Plain detached child (current main) | ❌ No — killed by `KillMode=control-group` | ❌ Never created |

### Security: env via spawn(), not argv

```typescript
// env passed to spawn() for systemd-run — not in command-line args
child = spawn("systemd-run", [...], { cwd: params.root, env, detached: true, stdio: "ignore" });
```

Test coverage verifies explicitly:
```typescript
expect(options.env).toBeDefined();
expect(args).not.toContain("--setenv");
```

### What was not tested

- Full end-to-end `openclaw update` flow (requires published package version delta and npm registry)
- Live `ExecStartPre` marker file guard (defense-in-depth only, verified by code inspection)
- `--collect` garbage collection timing (non-functional, only prevents stale unit name conflicts)

Closes #84068
