commands.author: Jerry-Xin

target.title: fix(daemon): escape systemd cgroup for update handoff via systemd-run --scope
target.body:
## Summary

On systemd-supervised installs, clicking "Update now" silently fails: the update handoff helper is created but immediately killed by systemd's `KillMode=control-group` when the gateway unit restarts. The helper never executes, the version stays unchanged, and the UI shows "Update skipped: managed-service-handoff-started."

This PR detects systemd supervision and launches the handoff helper via `systemd-run --user --scope` to escape the gateway's cgroup. Non-systemd supervisors (launchd, etc.) continue using the existing detached-child path.

## Root cause

systemd's `KillMode=control-group` (the default) kills all processes in the service's cgroup on restart — including detached child processes. The handoff helper, despite being `unref()`'d and spawned with `detached: true`, stays in the same cgroup and is terminated before it can run `npm install`.

## Fix

```
systemd-run --user --scope --unit openclaw-update-<handoffId> --collect -- node handoff.cjs handoff.json
```

Key design choices:

**`--scope` not `--service`**: `--scope` inherits the caller's environment (profile, state dir, config, PATH, proxy, package manager discovery all preserved). `--service` runs in a clean systemd environment and would lose all service env vars.

**`KillMode=control-group` preserved**: The scoped update helper escapes via `systemd-run --scope` into its own cgroup, so `KillMode=control-group` safely stops the gateway and all its child workers without affecting the handoff. No change to existing shutdown semantics.

**Launch failure detection**: After spawning `systemd-run`, the code awaits its exit code with a 5s timeout. If systemd-run exits non-zero (user session unavailable, D-Bus down, etc.), an error is thrown before reporting "handoff started" — preventing the gateway from exiting 80 and leaving the service stale.

**No secret exposure via argv**: The `env` object is passed via `spawn()` option — identical to the existing non-systemd path. No `--setenv=KEY=*** args are used. Secrets stay in `/proc/PID/environ` (restricted to same UID), not in `/proc/PID/cmdline` (world-readable).

Additional hardening:
- `sanitizeSystemdUnitName()` strips non-`[a-zA-Z0-9:._-]` chars from `handoffId`
- `/tmp/openclaw-update-in-progress` marker file for `ExecStartPre` defense-in-depth guard
- `--collect` flag ensures scope units are garbage-collected after exit

## Changes

| File | Change |
|------|--------|
| `update-managed-service-handoff.ts` | Detect `supervisor === "systemd"` → spawn via `systemd-run --user --scope`; await exit code to detect launch failures; add marker file lifecycle; add `sanitizeSystemdUnitName()` |
| `update-managed-service-handoff.test.ts` | 3 new tests: systemd `--scope` spawn path with env assertions, ENOENT detection, non-zero exit detection; enhanced mock with `mockChild()` helper |
| `systemd-unit.ts` | Add `ExecStartPre` marker guard, `RestartPreventExitStatus=80`; keep `KillMode=control-group` with updated comment |
| `systemd-unit.test.ts` | Updated KillMode assertion to verify `control-group` |

## Tests

```
$ npx vitest run --project gateway-methods src/gateway/server-methods/update-managed-service-handoff.test.ts
 ✓ gateway-methods (8 tests) 632ms
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  8 passed (8)

$ npx vitest run --project unit-fast src/daemon/systemd-unit.test.ts
 ✓ unit-fast (4 tests) 2ms
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  4 passed (4)
```

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: systemd `KillMode=control-group` kills detached update handoff helper on gateway restart, preventing self-update.
- **Real environment tested**: OrbStack Linux VM (Ubuntu 22.04 Jammy, kernel 6.19.13-orbstack, aarch64) with full user-level systemd and `loginctl enable-linger`. Node.js v24.15.0.

### Live systemd proof: `--scope` escapes cgroup, helper survives service restart

Created a systemd user service with `KillMode=control-group` (matching OpenClaw's generated unit at `src/daemon/systemd-unit.ts:86`):

```ini
[Service]
Type=simple
ExecStart=/tmp/handoff-test/gateway-sim.sh
KillMode=control-group
Restart=no
```

**Test A: WITH `systemd-run --user --scope` (this PR)**

```
=== Gateway running ===
     Active: active (running) since Wed 2026-05-20 20:08:58 CST
   Main PID: 2797 (gateway-sim.sh)
     CGroup: /user.slice/user-1000.slice/user@1000.service/app.slice/openclaw-gateway-test.service

=== Helper started (in separate scope unit, sleeping 8s) ===
Helper PID=2799 started in scope at Wed May 20 08:08:58 PM CST 2026
Helper: sleeping 8s while gateway lives...

=== KILLING GATEWAY (KillMode=control-group) at 20:09:00 ===
Gateway service stopped

=== Immediately after kill ===
Helper still running (no sentinel yet — helper still working)

=== After 10s wait (helper finished at 20:09:06) ===
Helper: still alive after 8s! Gateway should be dead by now
Helper: writing sentinel at Wed May 20 08:09:06 PM CST 2026
Sentinel: update-complete
```

**Result**: Helper survived gateway service kill and completed work ✅

**Test B: WITHOUT `systemd-run --scope` (current main behavior)**

Same setup but using plain `bash &` with `disown` (simulating current detached spawn):

```
=== Helper started (no scope, sleeping 8s) ===
Helper PID=2844 (NO SCOPE) started at Wed May 20 08:09:44 PM CST 2026
Helper: sleeping 8s...

=== KILLING GATEWAY (KillMode=control-group) at 20:09:46 ===

=== After 10s wait ===
Helper log: stopped at "sleeping 8s..." — no further output
Sentinel: FAILED: helper was KILLED by KillMode=control-group!
```

**Result**: Helper was killed when gateway service stopped ❌

### Comparison

| Scenario | Helper survives service stop? | Sentinel written? |
|----------|------------------------------|-------------------|
| `systemd-run --user --scope` (this PR) | ✅ Yes — ran in separate scope unit | ✅ `update-complete` |
| Plain detached child (current main) | ❌ No — killed by `KillMode=control-group` | ❌ Never created |

### Security: env via spawn(), not argv

```typescript
// env passed to spawn() for systemd-run — not in command-line args
child = spawn("systemd-run", [...], { cwd: params.root, env, detached: true, stdio: "ignore" });
```

Test coverage verifies explicitly:
```typescript
expect(options.env).toBeDefined();
expect(args).not.toContain("--setenv");
```

### What was not tested

- Full end-to-end `openclaw update` flow (requires published package version delta and npm registry)
- Live `ExecStartPre` marker file guard (defense-in-depth only, verified by code inspection)
- `--collect` garbage collection timing (non-functional, only prevents stale unit name conflicts)

Closes #84068

commands.author: 100yenadmin

target.title: [codex] make workspace prompt routing selectable
target.body:
## Summary This rebuild turns the Codex workspace prompt routing from a forced token-efficiency change into an explicit, user-selectable prompt-surface policy. - Adds `plugins.entries.codex.config.workspacePromptSurface`: - `per_turn_context` (default): quality-first legacy behavior. `SOUL.md`, `IDENTITY.md`, `TOOLS.md`, `USER.md`, `MEMORY.md`, and active `BOOTSTRAP.md` remain fresh current-turn context. - `thread_developer`: opt-in token-efficient/high-priority mode. Only stable profile files (`SOUL.md`, `IDENTITY.md`, `TOOLS.md`, `USER.md`) move to Codex thread developer instructions. - Adds `plugins.entries.codex.config.personalityMode`: - `soul_when_present` (default): when non-empty `SOUL.md` is delivered, send Codex `personality: "none"` and let the OpenClaw bridge point personality/tone/voice at `SOUL.md`. - `codex`: keep Codex's native personality overlay. - `none`: always disable Codex's named native personality overlay. - Keeps `AGENTS.md` / `AGENTS.override.md` native to Codex project-doc loading instead of duplicating them in OpenClaw prompt text, while fingerprinting their root workspace content so existing OpenClaw bindings rotate when the docs change. - Removes the lightweight Codex `project_doc_max_bytes: 0` suppression so cron/lightweight Codex runs preserve exact user input without disabling native project docs. Lightweight exact-command runs also do not disable Codex's native personality just because `SOUL.md` exists on disk; the SOUL override applies only when SOUL context is actually delivered. - Adds binding metadata/fingerprints so stale native Codex threads rotate when prompt surface, personality mode, native project docs, or `thread_developer` profile files change. ## Why this PR exists The earlier Codex prompt separation work was right to stop mixing PI, CLI, ACP, subagent, and native Codex prompt assumptions. Native Codex should keep its own base/model contract, while OpenClaw should add only the host-runtime bridge it owns: message delivery, dynamic OpenClaw tools, ACP/OpenClaw delegation boundaries, and runtime/channel context. The next optimization moved `SOUL.md`, `IDENTITY.md`, `TOOLS.md`, and `USER.md` out of every turn and into Codex developer instructions. That was attractive for token efficiency, but the adversarial review found a real quality/regression risk: users edit soul/profile/memory files during long-running OpenClaw sessions, and native Codex thread developer instructions are not a per-turn live-reload surface. Once a native Codex thread is already bound, resume-time developer/personality changes can be ignored by the loaded thread unless OpenClaw intentionally rotates the binding. The final direction here is: quality is the default, token efficiency is a choice. ## Prompt shape ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Codex base/model instructions"] --> B["Codex native personality slot"] B --> C["OpenClaw Codex developer bridge"] C --> D{"workspacePromptSurface"} D -->|"per_turn_context (default)"| E["SOUL / IDENTITY / TOOLS / USER
MEMORY / active BOOTSTRAP
fresh current-turn context"] D -->|"thread_developer (opt-in)"| F["SOUL / IDENTITY / TOOLS / USER
Codex thread developer instructions"] E --> G["Current user request"] F --> G H["AGENTS.md / AGENTS.override.md"] --> I["Codex native project-doc loader"] I --> G J["HEARTBEAT.md"] --> K["Heartbeat collaboration-mode pointer"] K --> G ``` The bridge text is intentionally narrow. It says OpenClaw is the host runtime and Codex is the native execution harness, that OpenClaw may prepend current-turn workspace/memory/skill/channel context, and that this context is reference material for the current turn rather than a replacement for Codex system/developer instructions. It also explicitly avoids treating every OpenClaw turn as coding work. When non-empty `SOUL.md` is delivered and `personalityMode` is left at the default, Codex receives the protocol enum `personality: "none"`. This is not model-visible text that says "have no personality"; Codex's protocol describes `none` as an empty native personality placeholder. The positive personality source remains `SOUL.md` via the OpenClaw bridge. Empty placeholder SOUL files keep Codex's native personality behavior. ## What we learned ```mermaid flowchart LR A["Original legacy behavior
fresh per-turn soul/profile context"] --> B["Prompt separation
Codex gets Codex lane, PI gets PI lane"] B --> C["Token optimization
stable files moved to developerInstructions"] C --> D["Adversarial finding
developerInstructions are not reliable live-refresh for existing native threads"] D --> E["This PR
default legacy quality + opt-in thread developer mode + binding rotation"] ``` Key findings from the review: - `SOUL.md` is personality, not collaboration mode. `collaborationMode.settings.developer_instructions` is too strong and mode-oriented for general soul/profile content; it remains appropriate for heartbeat/cron pointer behavior. - `AGENTS.md` and `AGENTS.override.md` are Codex project-doc concerns. OpenClaw should not duplicate them into turn text, but it should stop suppressing native loading and rotate OpenClaw's native Codex binding when loaded project-doc content changes. - `MEMORY.md`, active `BOOTSTRAP.md`, and skills are volatile enough to remain per-turn context even in the token-efficient mode. - `thread_developer` needs freshness metadata. If users opt into it, profile-file edits must rotate the native Codex thread rather than silently relying on resume-time overrides. - The default should preserve the legacy quality/freshness behavior because token savings should not silently outweigh agent identity quality. OpenAI's Codex prompt write-up describes native project-doc loading with `AGENTS.override.md`, `AGENTS.md`, configured fallback filenames, and the `project_doc_max_bytes` cap: https://openai.com/index/unrolling-the-codex-agent-loop/ ## Authority and lifetime | Surface | Owner | Files / content | Lifetime | This PR | | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | | Codex base/model instructions | Codex | Codex system/model stack | Native thread | OpenClaw does not override | | Codex personality slot | Codex protocol | `friendly`, `pragmatic`, `none` | Native thread/turn param | Configurable via `personalityMode`; defaults to `none` only when non-empty `SOUL.md` is delivered | | OpenClaw developer bridge | OpenClaw | Runtime/delivery/tool/delegation bridge | Native thread | Kept narrow and host-owned | | OpenClaw per-turn context | OpenClaw | Soul/profile/memory/bootstrap/skills/channel context | Every turn | Default quality-first surface | | Codex thread developer instructions | OpenClaw -> Codex | Stable soul/profile files only | Native thread | Opt-in `thread_developer`, with fingerprint rotation | | Native Codex project docs | Codex | `AGENTS.md`, `AGENTS.override.md`, fallbacks | Native project-doc load | Preserved; no `project_doc_max_bytes: 0` suppression | | Heartbeat collaboration pointer | OpenClaw | Pointer to `HEARTBEAT.md` | Heartbeat turn | Still separate from soul/profile | ## Scenario coverage | Scenario | Expected behavior | | --- | --- | | Default Codex app-server run with non-empty `SOUL.md` | Stable profile files are current-turn context; Codex native personality is disabled; bridge points personality at `SOUL.md`. | | Default Codex app-server run with empty `SOUL.md` | Empty placeholder content does not suppress Codex's native personality overlay. | | User edits `SOUL.md` mid-session in default mode | Next turn sees the edit because the file remains per-turn context. | | User opts into `thread_developer` | Stable profile files move to developer instructions; `MEMORY.md` and active `BOOTSTRAP.md` remain turn context. | | User edits `SOUL.md` in `thread_developer` mode | OpenClaw fingerprints the tracked developer files and starts a fresh native Codex thread. | | User edits `AGENTS.md` / `AGENTS.override.md` | OpenClaw fingerprints loaded native project-doc content and rotates the native Codex binding. | | Lightweight cron Codex run | OpenClaw still skips extra bootstrap prompt text for exact command preservation, does not disable native Codex project docs, and does not send `personality: "none"` unless the user explicitly configured `personalityMode: "none"`. | | Existing stale binding from the previous PR | Missing prompt-surface metadata forces a new native Codex thread, so old bindings do not preserve stale developer/user context. | | Manual `/codex resume ` binding | The minimal manual binding resumes once, then persists the current prompt-surface/personality metadata after successful resume. | ## Implementation notes - `extensions/codex/src/app-server/config.ts` adds the new config enums and resolvers. - `extensions/codex/src/app-server/run-attempt.ts` selects the workspace prompt surface, computes the workspace fingerprint, chooses the positive SOUL bridge behavior, and keeps native project docs out of OpenClaw turn text. - `extensions/codex/src/app-server/thread-lifecycle.ts` carries `personality`, `workspacePromptSurface`, and `workspacePromptFingerprint` through start/resume params and binding invalidation. - `extensions/codex/src/app-server/session-binding.ts` persists the prompt-surface metadata. - Docs now describe the architecture in `docs/plugins/codex-harness-runtime.md`, `docs/plugins/codex-harness-reference.md`, and `docs/concepts/system-prompt.md`. ## Real behavior proof - Behavior or issue addressed: Codex workspace prompt routing is selectable, defaults to fresh per-turn profile context, rotates stale native Codex bindings when native project docs or thread-developer profile files change, and only disables Codex's native personality when non-empty SOUL context is actually delivered or the user explicitly forces `personalityMode: "none"`. - Current narrowed head tested: `ad037cc543` on branch `codex/workspace-context-routing-v2`, rebased on `upstream/main` `a13468320c`. - Scope note: this head removes the prior fs-safe default flip, archive fallback, Windows ACL policy change, and dependency expectation cleanup from the PR. The only non-Codex file left is `extensions/openrouter/provider-routing.ts`, a behavior-preserving object-spread lint cleanup required because the repo's fail-safe changed gate lints the extension surface on this branch. - Real environment tested: local OpenClaw checkout on macOS under `/Volumes/LEXAR/repos/openclaw-codex-workspace-routing`, using a temporary OpenClaw gateway and Codex app-server model route. - Exact steps or command run after this patch: ```bash OPENCLAW_LIVE=1 \ OPENCLAW_LIVE_CODEX_HARNESS=1 \ OPENCLAW_LIVE_CODEX_HARNESS_MODEL=codex/gpt-5.4-mini \ OPENCLAW_LIVE_CODEX_HARNESS_DEBUG=1 \ OPENCLAW_LIVE_CODEX_HARNESS_REQUEST_TIMEOUT_MS=300000 \ node scripts/test-live.mjs -- src/gateway/gateway-codex-harness.live.test.ts -t "project_doc_max_bytes" ``` - Evidence after fix: ```text [gateway-codex-live] project-doc-budget-binding-proof { "defaultHiddenEditKeptThread": true, "hiddenEditKeptThread": true, "visibleEditRotatedThread": true, "defaultFirst": { "threadId": "019e45f3-27a2-7f52-a28c-1cb464d20aa3", "workspacePromptFingerprint": "7040706c03c9a003b0d91a16dd33cb5dbe0f7e3c039e9042ed3e13b11765f15b", "workspacePromptSurface": "per_turn_context" }, "defaultHidden": { "threadId": "019e45f3-27a2-7f52-a28c-1cb464d20aa3", "workspacePromptFingerprint": "7040706c03c9a003b0d91a16dd33cb5dbe0f7e3c039e9042ed3e13b11765f15b", "workspacePromptSurface": "per_turn_context" }, "first": { "threadId": "019e45f3-4e9f-79d1-bb96-bfac5501bebb", "workspacePromptFingerprint": "67ae5644fa63c69b79a5bd91692a5a9eb276eb1921952c0f14aae55848cf9871", "workspacePromptSurface": "per_turn_context" }, "hidden": { "threadId": "019e45f3-4e9f-79d1-bb96-bfac5501bebb", "workspacePromptFingerprint": "67ae5644fa63c69b79a5bd91692a5a9eb276eb1921952c0f14aae55848cf9871", "workspacePromptSurface": "per_turn_context" }, "visible": { "threadId": "019e45f3-6951-7eb0-83ab-5b009ececa6f", "workspacePromptFingerprint": "1c20f3c6e3ba646eb74b03e4b75a176df0b1494bc095406685c7e6be74275508", "workspacePromptSurface": "per_turn_context" } } Test Files 1 passed (1) Tests 1 passed | 2 skipped (3) Duration 94.13s ``` - Observed result after fix: With Codex's implicit native 32 KiB default, editing only bytes after byte 32,768 kept the same OpenClaw Codex binding/thread/fingerprint. With `project_doc_max_bytes = 8`, editing only bytes outside the explicit native budget kept the same binding/thread/fingerprint, while editing bytes inside the native budget rotated both the native thread and workspace prompt fingerprint. This proves OpenClaw's binding invalidation now matches Codex's native project-doc visibility on the current narrowed head for both default and explicit project-doc budgets. - What was not tested: Broad cross-platform and full-matrix coverage are left to GitHub Actions; the live proof used local macOS Codex app-server auth/model access. ## Validation Current-head focused validation from `/Volumes/LEXAR/repos/openclaw-codex-workspace-routing`: ```bash OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 node scripts/run-vitest.mjs \ extensions/codex/src/app-server/run-attempt.test.ts \ -t "project_doc_max_bytes|default project-doc budget" ``` Result: 1 test file passed, 3 tests passed, 195 skipped. Current-head live Codex harness proof: ```bash OPENCLAW_LIVE=1 \ OPENCLAW_LIVE_CODEX_HARNESS=1 \ OPENCLAW_LIVE_CODEX_HARNESS_MODEL=codex/gpt-5.4-mini \ OPENCLAW_LIVE_CODEX_HARNESS_DEBUG=1 \ OPENCLAW_LIVE_CODEX_HARNESS_REQUEST_TIMEOUT_MS=300000 \ node scripts/test-live.mjs -- src/gateway/gateway-codex-harness.live.test.ts -t "project_doc_max_bytes" ``` Result: 1 test file passed, 1 test passed, 2 skipped. Current-head formatting/check validation: ```bash pnpm exec oxfmt --check \ extensions/codex/src/app-server/run-attempt.ts \ extensions/codex/src/app-server/run-attempt.test.ts \ src/gateway/gateway-codex-harness.live.test.ts git diff --check ``` Result: passed. Changed-gate validation: ```bash node scripts/run-oxlint-shards.mjs -- extensions/openrouter/provider-routing.ts pnpm check:changed ``` Result: passed. `pnpm check:changed` completed typecheck, lint, dependency/package guards, duplicate coverage, runtime sidecar loader guard, conflict-marker/changelog checks, and runtime import-cycle checks. ## Maintainer review notes This PR intentionally keeps the Codex-native lane narrow: - It does not change harness selection policy, model routing, auth routing, or Codex app-server transport behavior. - It preserves native Codex base/model instructions and native project-doc loading instead of replacing them with OpenClaw prompt text. - It keeps quality-first per-turn profile context as the default and makes token-efficient thread developer instructions opt-in. - It rotates native Codex bindings when the selected prompt surface, personality policy, visible native project docs, or `thread_developer` profile files change. - It leaves volatile `MEMORY.md`, active `BOOTSTRAP.md`, and skill context in the per-turn lane even when stable soul/profile files are opted into thread developer instructions.

target.title: Policy: add secrets auth provenance checks
target.body:
## Summary

Adds a Policy 1.0 secrets/auth provenance category with redacted evidence and doctor/lint findings. Stacked after #81104.

- Records secret provider/input provenance without exposing secret values.
- Enforces `secrets.disallowInline`, `secrets.requireManagedProviders`, `secrets.denySources`, and `secrets.allowInsecureProviders`.
- Adds auth profile metadata/mode checks with `auth.profiles.requireMetadata` and `auth.profiles.allowModes`.
- Documents the new policy rules and JSON evidence shape.

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior addressed: adds policy conformance evidence and findings for configured secret/auth provenance without exposing raw secret material, including inline secrets, unmanaged SecretRef providers, denied provider sources, insecure secret providers, missing auth-profile metadata, and unapproved auth-profile modes.
- Real environment tested: WSL Ubuntu-24.04 source worktrees created from `upstream/main` and PR head `ac8f697003556d3a15f984eb7b7edbf4a076c024`.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: created clean detached worktrees for `upstream/main` and `fork/policy-secrets-auth-provenance`; in each worktree ran `node --import ./node_modules/tsx/dist/loader.mjs ./probe-secrets-auth-policy.mjs`. The probe wrote temp `openclaw.jsonc` and `policy.jsonc` files, registered the real policy doctor checks with `registerPolicyDoctorChecks()`, and ran the real doctor/lint path with `runDoctorLintChecks()` against a config containing a sentinel inline secret, an unmanaged env SecretRef, an `exec` secret provider, an insecure file provider, and auth profile policy violations.
- Evidence after fix (screenshot, recording, terminal capture, console output, redacted runtime log, linked artifact, or copied live output):

![PR #81974 secrets/auth provenance before/after proof](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/giodl73-repo/openclaw/proof-artifacts/pr-81974-fresh/pr-81974/pr-81974-secrets-auth-provenance-before-after-proof.png)

```text
Fresh proof artifact: https://raw.githubusercontent.com/giodl73-repo/openclaw/proof-artifacts/pr-81974-fresh/pr-81974/pr-81974-secrets-auth-provenance-before-after-proof.png
Proof summary: https://raw.githubusercontent.com/giodl73-repo/openclaw/proof-artifacts/pr-81974-fresh/pr-81974/summary.txt

policyRegisterImport=missing-or-failed

AFTER PR #81974 head:

```

- Observed result after fix: `upstream/main` has no secrets/auth policy registration path, so no relevant checks or findings are produced. PR head registers 6 secrets/auth policy checks, reports 6 denied posture findings, and keeps the sentinel inline secret out of serialized findings.
- What was not tested: no live external vault or credential provider was contacted. The proof uses local temp config/policy files and verifies the policy doctor/lint path plus redaction behavior.

target.title: Audit secret-bearing backup files
target.body:
## Summary
- extend `secrets audit` to scan sibling backup files for `.env`, `auth-profiles.json`, and generated `models.json`
- detect plaintext credential residue in `.bak` / `.bak.*` backup naming patterns without resolving historical backup-only SecretRefs
- keep `openclaw.json` config backup scanning out of this PR so it can remain aligned with #83275's config artifact scanner
- add coverage for env, auth profile, models backup residues, numbered backup files, and backup-only SecretRef cases

## Context
This addresses a remaining API-key exposure surface discussed in #11829: historical backup files can retain plaintext credentials even after users migrate active config to SecretRefs.

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior or issue addressed:** `openclaw secrets audit --json` now scans secret-bearing backup siblings for `.env`, `auth-profiles.json`, and generated `models.json`, and reports plaintext residues from those backup files. It does not scan `openclaw.json` config backups in this PR because #83275 owns that config-artifact surface.

**Real environment tested:** Local source checkout on macOS, Node.js v25.6.1, pnpm 11.1.0 via `npx pnpm@11.1.0`. The run used a temporary OpenClaw state directory with only redacted dummy values.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch:**
1. Created a temporary `OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR` with active `openclaw.json`, empty `auth-profiles.json`, and generated `models.json`.
2. Added redacted backup files: `.env.bak.1`, `auth-profiles.json.bak.1`, and `models.json.bak.1`.
3. Ran:

```bash
OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR="$TMP/state" \
OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH="$TMP/state/openclaw.json" \
OPENAI_API_KEY=sk-redacted-active \
npx pnpm@11.1.0 --silent openclaw secrets audit --json
```

**Evidence after fix:** Redacted console output from the real CLI run:

```json
{
  "status": "findings",
  "summary": {
    "plaintextCount": 3,
    "unresolvedRefCount": 0,
    "shadowedRefCount": 1,
    "legacyResidueCount": 0
  },
  "filesScanned": [
    "$TMP/state/.env.bak.1",
    "$TMP/state/agents/main/agent/auth-profiles.json",
    "$TMP/state/agents/main/agent/auth-profiles.json.bak.1",
    "$TMP/state/agents/main/agent/models.json",
    "$TMP/state/agents/main/agent/models.json.bak.1",
    "$TMP/state/openclaw.json"
  ],
  "backupFindings": [
    {
      "code": "PLAINTEXT_FOUND",
      "severity": "warn",
      "file": "$TMP/state/.env.bak.1",
      "jsonPath": "$env.OPENAI_API_KEY",
      "message": "Potential secret found in .env (OPENAI_API_KEY)."
    },
    {
      "code": "PLAINTEXT_FOUND",
      "severity": "warn",
      "file": "$TMP/state/agents/main/agent/auth-profiles.json.bak.1",
      "jsonPath": "profiles.openai:default.key",
      "message": "Auth profile API key is stored as plaintext."
    },
    {
      "code": "PLAINTEXT_FOUND",
      "severity": "warn",
      "file": "$TMP/state/agents/main/agent/models.json.bak.1",
      "jsonPath": "providers.openai.apiKey",
      "message": "models.json provider apiKey is stored as plaintext."
    }
  ]
}
```

**Observed result after fix:** The CLI returned `status: findings`, scanned all three backup files, and reported three backup plaintext findings with `unresolvedRefCount: 0`.

**What was not tested:** I did not test real user credentials, live providers, or `openclaw.json` config backup artifacts. The config backup artifact surface is intentionally left to #83275.

## Test
- `npx pnpm@11.1.0 vitest run src/secrets/audit.test.ts` -> 26 passed
- `npx pnpm@11.1.0 exec oxfmt --check src/secrets/storage-scan.ts src/secrets/audit.ts src/secrets/audit.test.ts`
- `npx pnpm@11.1.0 exec oxlint src/secrets/storage-scan.ts src/secrets/audit.ts src/secrets/audit.test.ts`
- `git diff --check`

target.title: fix(browser): honor image sanitization config for screenshots
target.body:
## Summary
- forward `agents.defaults.imageMaxDimensionPx` into browser screenshot tool results
- forward the same image sanitization limits into `snapshot --labels` image results
- add regression coverage for both browser image-returning paths

## Problem
`browser screenshot` normalized images to the browser layer's `<= 2000px` limit first, but then returned them through `imageResultFromFile()` without passing runtime image sanitization config.

That meant the final tool image still fell back to the default `1200px` sanitize limit, even when `agents.defaults.imageMaxDimensionPx` was configured higher. The same omission existed for `browser snapshot --labels`.

## Fix
Thread runtime image sanitization limits into both browser image-returning call sites:
- `browser:screenshot`
- `browser:snapshot` when `labels=true`

This keeps browser tool image behavior aligned with the documented global image sanitization setting and with other OpenClaw image paths that already forward this config.

## Verification
- focused browser extension test suite passed:
  - `extensions/browser/src/browser-tool.test.ts` (`51 passed`)
- direct runtime check with the same input image showed:
  - default config: final browser screenshot output still reduced to `1200x625`
  - `imageMaxDimensionPx = 1920`: final output became `1920x1000`
  - `imageMaxDimensionPx = 2000`: final output stayed at `2000x1042`
  - `imageMaxDimensionPx = 3000`: final output still stayed at `2000x1042`, as expected because browser pre-normalization already caps screenshots at `2000px`

## Real behavior proof
**Behavior or issue addressed**: browser screenshot and `browser snapshot --labels` image results ignored `agents.defaults.imageMaxDimensionPx` on the final tool-image sanitize step, so a browser screenshot could still come out at `1200px` even when the config was set higher.

**Real environment tested**: local OpenClaw checkout on macOS using the real browser screenshot normalization path plus the browser tool image-result path with a local PNG test image as the input.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch**: ran the browser screenshot runtime check against the patched branch, varying `agents.defaults.imageMaxDimensionPx` across default, `1920`, `2000`, and `3000`, then inspected the generated image metadata.

**Evidence after fix**:
```text
live output from runtime image metadata inspection:
normalized browser layer: 2000x1042, 246537 bytes, image/jpeg
default final output:    1200x625,  107491 bytes, image/jpeg
configured 1920:         1920x1000, 214162 bytes, image/jpeg
configured 2000:         2000x1042, 246537 bytes, image/jpeg
configured 3000:         2000x1042, 246537 bytes, image/jpeg
```

**Observed result after fix**: after the patch, browser-originated image results now honor the configured sanitize limit up to the browser layer's own `2000px` ceiling. `1920` produces `1920x1000`, `2000` produces `2000x1042`, and `3000` does not exceed `2000x1042` because browser pre-normalization already caps that path.

**What was not tested**: a full remote provider call was not run; this proof covers the local browser screenshot normalization path and the actual browser tool image packaging and sanitize path.

target.title: fix(minimax): stop advertising music duration control
target.body:
## Summary

- Stop MiniMax music generation from advertising `durationSeconds` support because the official MiniMax `/v1/music_generation` request contract does not document a duration field.
- Send the MiniMax prompt as authored instead of appending a duration hint that the provider cannot enforce.
- Update MiniMax provider tests and music generation docs so the exposed controls match runtime behavior.

Fixes #84508

## Root Cause

The MiniMax music provider claimed `supportsDuration: true`, but `durationSeconds` was only converted into prompt text. That made the shared `music_generate` capability advertise a runtime control that MiniMax does not actually enforce through a documented structured API field.

## Why This Is Safe

This narrows the advertised MiniMax capability to match the vendor API contract and existing runtime behavior. Callers that request duration through the shared music generation runtime now get the normal unsupported-override handling instead of a misleading MiniMax-specific prompt hint.

Security/runtime controls are unchanged: provider authentication, HTTP request configuration, private-network blocking, response validation, download handling, and mp3-only format validation are untouched.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: MiniMax music generation no longer advertises durationSeconds support because the official MiniMax `/v1/music_generation` API contract does not document a duration request field.

Real environment tested: Local macOS checkout with `MINIMAX_API_KEY` configured from `.env`, using the patched provider code. Secret values were not printed; the live provider log is redacted.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```bash
node --import tsx --input-type=module - <<'NODE'
import { buildMinimaxMusicGenerationProvider } from './extensions/minimax/music-generation-provider.ts';
import { resolveMusicGenerationOverrides } from './src/music-generation/normalization.ts';

const provider = buildMinimaxMusicGenerationProvider();
const generateCaps = provider.capabilities.generate;
const resolved = resolveMusicGenerationOverrides({
  provider,
  model: 'music-2.6',
  durationSeconds: 30,
  format: 'mp3',
});

console.log(JSON.stringify({
  provider: provider.id,
  model: 'music-2.6',
  advertisedControls: {
    supportsLyrics: generateCaps?.supportsLyrics === true,
    supportsInstrumental: generateCaps?.supportsInstrumental === true,
    supportsDuration: generateCaps?.supportsDuration === true,
    supportsFormat: generateCaps?.supportsFormat === true,
    supportedFormats: generateCaps?.supportedFormats ?? [],
  },
  requestedOverrides: { durationSeconds: 30, format: 'mp3' },
  sanitizedRequest: {
    durationSeconds: resolved.durationSeconds ?? null,
    format: resolved.format ?? null,
  },
  ignoredOverrides: resolved.ignoredOverrides,
}, null, 2));
NODE

pnpm test extensions/minimax/music-generation-provider.test.ts
OPENCLAW_LIVE_TEST=1 OPENCLAW_LIVE_TEST_QUIET=0 OPENCLAW_LIVE_MUSIC_GENERATION_PROVIDERS=minimax pnpm test:live -- extensions/music-generation-providers.live.test.ts --reporter=verbose
```

Capability and ignored-override evidence after fix:

```json
{
  "provider": "minimax",
  "model": "music-2.6",
  "advertisedControls": {
    "supportsLyrics": true,
    "supportsInstrumental": true,
    "supportsDuration": false,
    "supportsFormat": true,
    "supportedFormats": [
      "mp3"
    ]
  },
  "requestedOverrides": {
    "durationSeconds": 30,
    "format": "mp3"
  },
  "sanitizedRequest": {
    "durationSeconds": null,
    "format": "mp3"
  },
  "ignoredOverrides": [
    {
      "key": "durationSeconds",
      "value": 30
    }
  ]
}
```

Live MiniMax generation evidence after fix:

```text
$ node scripts/test-live.mjs -- extensions/music-generation-providers.live.test.ts --reporter=verbose

 RUN  v4.1.6 /Users/neeravmakwana/Desktop/github_repos/openclaw_repo_prrefresh

[test:live] still running (40s elapsed, 39s since last output)
[test:live] still running (60s elapsed, 20s since last output)
[live:music-generation] attempted=minimax:generate:music-2.6 (env: MINIMAX_API_KEY [redacted]) skipped=none failures=none shellEnv=none
 ✓ extensions/music-generation-providers.live.test.ts > music generation provider live > covers generate plus declared edit paths with shell/profile auth 60367ms

 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  1 passed (1)
   Start at  09:17:15
   Duration  68.90s (transform 6.20s, setup 237ms, import 8.17s, tests 60.37s, environment 0ms)
```

Observed result after fix: MiniMax no longer advertises duration support, the shared runtime normalization drops `durationSeconds` and records it as an ignored override, and MiniMax still generates an audio track successfully through the live provider path.

What was not tested: I did not verify MiniMax duration control because the official MiniMax API reference does not expose a documented duration request parameter.

## Verification

- `pnpm docs:list`
- `git diff --check`
- `pnpm test extensions/minimax/music-generation-provider.test.ts`
- `pnpm check:changed`
- `node --import tsx --input-type=module ...` capability/ignored-override proof shown above
- `OPENCLAW_LIVE_TEST=1 OPENCLAW_LIVE_TEST_QUIET=0 OPENCLAW_LIVE_MUSIC_GENERATION_PROVIDERS=minimax pnpm test:live -- extensions/music-generation-providers.live.test.ts --reporter=verbose`

## Out of Scope

- Adding a MiniMax duration request field if MiniMax later documents one.
- Refactoring MiniMax music generation into async task polling.
- Changing other music providers that already have documented duration controls.

commands.author: Jerry-Xin

target.title: fix(infra): tolerate chmod EPERM on K8s fsGroup-mounted PVC
target.body:
## Summary

On Kubernetes deployments with `fsGroup`-mounted PVCs, `chmod(dir, 0o700)` fails with EPERM because the state directory is owned by root (with group ownership set to the fsGroup GID). This crashes exec approvals, task registry, task-flow registry, and plugin-state initialization on startup.

This PR extracts a shared `tryChmodSync` helper that:
1. Tries `chmodSync(target, mode)` as before
2. On EPERM/EACCES/EROFS: verifies the process can actually access the path via `accessSync`
3. If accessible → tolerates the failure (the fsGroup grants adequate permissions via group bits)
4. If not accessible → rethrows the original error

## Root cause

```
EPERM: operation not permitted, chmod '/home/openclaw/.openclaw'
```

K8s `fsGroup` volumes are owned by `root:<fsGroup>` with setgid bit. The container process runs as a non-root UID in the fsGroup, so it has full access via group membership but cannot `chmodSync` a root-owned directory.

## Changes

| File | Change |
|------|--------|
| `src/infra/chmod.ts` | New `tryChmodSync` helper — chmod with EPERM/EACCES/EROFS tolerance |
| `src/infra/chmod.test.ts` | 9 tests covering all error codes and access verification paths |
| `src/infra/exec-approvals.ts` | Use `tryChmodSync` instead of raw `chmodSync` |
| `src/tasks/task-registry.store.sqlite.ts` | Use `tryChmodSync` |
| `src/tasks/task-flow-registry.store.sqlite.ts` | Use `tryChmodSync` |
| `src/plugin-state/plugin-state-store.sqlite.ts` | Use `tryChmodSync` |

## Tests

```
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/infra/chmod.test.ts
 ✓ infra  ../../src/infra/chmod.test.ts (12 tests) 16ms
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  12 passed (12)
   Duration  152ms
```

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: `chmod(dir, 0o700)` throws EPERM on K8s fsGroup-mounted PVC, crashing exec approvals and state initialization. The helper tolerates EPERM when access is verified, and rejects only the genuinely dangerous case (unexpected other-WRITE permission).
- **Real environment tested**: Local patched OpenClaw checkout at `bfadbe3e37`, macOS arm64, Node.js `v24.10.0`. Also reproduced on OrbStack Kubernetes v1.33.9.
- **Failing proof before fix**: On upstream/main (`a002c416c7`), `chmodSync(dir, 0o700)` on a K8s fsGroup volume throws EPERM and crashes the gateway on startup. Test file `src/infra/chmod.test.ts` does not exist on upstream/main.

### Permission boundary policy (updated in `bfadbe3e37`)

K8s fsGroup volumes commonly set directories to mode `2775` (setgid + rwxrwxr-x). The helper now:
- **Accepts** `2775` (other has read+execute but NOT write) ✅
- **Accepts** `2755`, `2751`, etc. (other read/execute without write) ✅
- **Rejects** `2777` or any mode with unexpected other-WRITE (`0o002`) ❌ — this signals a genuine security misconfiguration

This boundary aligns with the real K8s fsGroup behavior: volume plugins set read+execute on `other`, but never write.

### Live K8s reproduction

Pod spec:
```yaml
securityContext:
  runAsUser: 1000
  runAsGroup: 1000
  fsGroup: 1000
volumes:
  - name: state
    emptyDir: {}  # simulates fsGroup PVC
```

Terminal output from `kubectl exec`:
```
=== K8s fsGroup EPERM Reproduction ===
Node: v24.15.0
User: uid=1000(node) gid=1000(node) groups=1000(node)

--- State directory (fsGroup mount) ---
drwxrwsr-x 1 root node  0 May 20 07:00 .openclaw

Owner: 0:1000
Mode: 0o2775

--- Main branch: chmod(dir, 0o700) ---
chmod FAILED: EPERM EPERM: operation not permitted, chmod '/home/openclaw/.openclaw'

--- PR fix: tryChmodSync(dir, 0o700) ---
chmod failed with EPERM - checking access...
accessSync(R_OK|W_OK|X_OK) PASSED ✅
actualOtherWrite=0, requestedOtherWrite=0 → tolerating
→ tryChmodSync tolerates EPERM, gateway starts normally

--- Verify: can create state files ---
Write+Read: ✅ {"test":true}
Cleanup: ✅
```

- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:

```text
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/infra/chmod.test.ts
```

- **Evidence after fix**:

```text
 ✓ infra  ../../src/infra/chmod.test.ts (12 tests) 16ms

 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  12 passed (12)
   Duration  152ms
```

- **Observed result after fix**: All 12 chmod tests pass — EPERM tolerance with accessSync fallback, K8s fsGroup mode 2775 accepted, world-writable (other-WRITE) rejected, and normal chmod behavior preserved.
- **What was not tested**: Full OpenClaw gateway startup inside a live K8s cluster. The chmod behavior is validated via mocked fs calls and real K8s `kubectl exec` reproduction.

Closes #83619

target.title: [codex] make workspace prompt routing selectable
target.body:
## Summary This rebuild turns the Codex workspace prompt routing from a forced token-efficiency change into an explicit, user-selectable prompt-surface policy. - Adds `plugins.entries.codex.config.workspacePromptSurface`: - `per_turn_context` (default): quality-first legacy behavior. `SOUL.md`, `IDENTITY.md`, `TOOLS.md`, `USER.md`, `MEMORY.md`, and active `BOOTSTRAP.md` remain fresh current-turn context. - `thread_developer`: opt-in token-efficient/high-priority mode. Only stable profile files (`SOUL.md`, `IDENTITY.md`, `TOOLS.md`, `USER.md`) move to Codex thread developer instructions. - Adds `plugins.entries.codex.config.personalityMode`: - `soul_when_present` (default): when non-empty `SOUL.md` is delivered, send Codex `personality: "none"` and let the OpenClaw bridge point personality/tone/voice at `SOUL.md`. - `codex`: keep Codex's native personality overlay. - `none`: always disable Codex's named native personality overlay. - Keeps `AGENTS.md` / `AGENTS.override.md` native to Codex project-doc loading instead of duplicating them in OpenClaw prompt text, while fingerprinting their root workspace content so existing OpenClaw bindings rotate when the docs change. - Removes the lightweight Codex `project_doc_max_bytes: 0` suppression so cron/lightweight Codex runs preserve exact user input without disabling native project docs. Lightweight exact-command runs also do not disable Codex's native personality just because `SOUL.md` exists on disk; the SOUL override applies only when SOUL context is actually delivered. - Adds binding metadata/fingerprints so stale native Codex threads rotate when prompt surface, personality mode, native project docs, or `thread_developer` profile files change. ## Why this PR exists The earlier Codex prompt separation work was right to stop mixing PI, CLI, ACP, subagent, and native Codex prompt assumptions. Native Codex should keep its own base/model contract, while OpenClaw should add only the host-runtime bridge it owns: message delivery, dynamic OpenClaw tools, ACP/OpenClaw delegation boundaries, and runtime/channel context. The next optimization moved `SOUL.md`, `IDENTITY.md`, `TOOLS.md`, and `USER.md` out of every turn and into Codex developer instructions. That was attractive for token efficiency, but the adversarial review found a real quality/regression risk: users edit soul/profile/memory files during long-running OpenClaw sessions, and native Codex thread developer instructions are not a per-turn live-reload surface. Once a native Codex thread is already bound, resume-time developer/personality changes can be ignored by the loaded thread unless OpenClaw intentionally rotates the binding. The final direction here is: quality is the default, token efficiency is a choice. ## Prompt shape ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Codex base/model instructions"] --> B["Codex native personality slot"] B --> C["OpenClaw Codex developer bridge"] C --> D{"workspacePromptSurface"} D -->|"per_turn_context (default)"| E["SOUL / IDENTITY / TOOLS / USER
MEMORY / active BOOTSTRAP
fresh current-turn context"] D -->|"thread_developer (opt-in)"| F["SOUL / IDENTITY / TOOLS / USER
Codex thread developer instructions"] E --> G["Current user request"] F --> G H["AGENTS.md / AGENTS.override.md"] --> I["Codex native project-doc loader"] I --> G J["HEARTBEAT.md"] --> K["Heartbeat collaboration-mode pointer"] K --> G ``` The bridge text is intentionally narrow. It says OpenClaw is the host runtime and Codex is the native execution harness, that OpenClaw may prepend current-turn workspace/memory/skill/channel context, and that this context is reference material for the current turn rather than a replacement for Codex system/developer instructions. It also explicitly avoids treating every OpenClaw turn as coding work. When non-empty `SOUL.md` is delivered and `personalityMode` is left at the default, Codex receives the protocol enum `personality: "none"`. This is not model-visible text that says "have no personality"; Codex's protocol describes `none` as an empty native personality placeholder. The positive personality source remains `SOUL.md` via the OpenClaw bridge. Empty placeholder SOUL files keep Codex's native personality behavior. ## What we learned ```mermaid flowchart LR A["Original legacy behavior
fresh per-turn soul/profile context"] --> B["Prompt separation
Codex gets Codex lane, PI gets PI lane"] B --> C["Token optimization
stable files moved to developerInstructions"] C --> D["Adversarial finding
developerInstructions are not reliable live-refresh for existing native threads"] D --> E["This PR
default legacy quality + opt-in thread developer mode + binding rotation"] ``` Key findings from the review: - `SOUL.md` is personality, not collaboration mode. `collaborationMode.settings.developer_instructions` is too strong and mode-oriented for general soul/profile content; it remains appropriate for heartbeat/cron pointer behavior. - `AGENTS.md` and `AGENTS.override.md` are Codex project-doc concerns. OpenClaw should not duplicate them into turn text, but it should stop suppressing native loading and rotate OpenClaw's native Codex binding when loaded project-doc content changes. - `MEMORY.md`, active `BOOTSTRAP.md`, and skills are volatile enough to remain per-turn context even in the token-efficient mode. - `thread_developer` needs freshness metadata. If users opt into it, profile-file edits must rotate the native Codex thread rather than silently relying on resume-time overrides. - The default should preserve the legacy quality/freshness behavior because token savings should not silently outweigh agent identity quality. OpenAI's Codex prompt write-up describes native project-doc loading with `AGENTS.override.md`, `AGENTS.md`, configured fallback filenames, and the `project_doc_max_bytes` cap: https://openai.com/index/unrolling-the-codex-agent-loop/ ## Authority and lifetime | Surface | Owner | Files / content | Lifetime | This PR | | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | | Codex base/model instructions | Codex | Codex system/model stack | Native thread | OpenClaw does not override | | Codex personality slot | Codex protocol | `friendly`, `pragmatic`, `none` | Native thread/turn param | Configurable via `personalityMode`; defaults to `none` only when non-empty `SOUL.md` is delivered | | OpenClaw developer bridge | OpenClaw | Runtime/delivery/tool/delegation bridge | Native thread | Kept narrow and host-owned | | OpenClaw per-turn context | OpenClaw | Soul/profile/memory/bootstrap/skills/channel context | Every turn | Default quality-first surface | | Codex thread developer instructions | OpenClaw -> Codex | Stable soul/profile files only | Native thread | Opt-in `thread_developer`, with fingerprint rotation | | Native Codex project docs | Codex | `AGENTS.md`, `AGENTS.override.md`, fallbacks | Native project-doc load | Preserved; no `project_doc_max_bytes: 0` suppression | | Heartbeat collaboration pointer | OpenClaw | Pointer to `HEARTBEAT.md` | Heartbeat turn | Still separate from soul/profile | ## Scenario coverage | Scenario | Expected behavior | | --- | --- | | Default Codex app-server run with non-empty `SOUL.md` | Stable profile files are current-turn context; Codex native personality is disabled; bridge points personality at `SOUL.md`. | | Default Codex app-server run with empty `SOUL.md` | Empty placeholder content does not suppress Codex's native personality overlay. | | User edits `SOUL.md` mid-session in default mode | Next turn sees the edit because the file remains per-turn context. | | User opts into `thread_developer` | Stable profile files move to developer instructions; `MEMORY.md` and active `BOOTSTRAP.md` remain turn context. | | User edits `SOUL.md` in `thread_developer` mode | OpenClaw fingerprints the tracked developer files and starts a fresh native Codex thread. | | User edits `AGENTS.md` / `AGENTS.override.md` | OpenClaw fingerprints loaded native project-doc content and rotates the native Codex binding. | | Lightweight cron Codex run | OpenClaw still skips extra bootstrap prompt text for exact command preservation, does not disable native Codex project docs, and does not send `personality: "none"` unless the user explicitly configured `personalityMode: "none"`. | | Existing stale binding from the previous PR | Missing prompt-surface metadata forces a new native Codex thread, so old bindings do not preserve stale developer/user context. | | Manual `/codex resume ` binding | The minimal manual binding resumes once, then persists the current prompt-surface/personality metadata after successful resume. | ## Implementation notes - `extensions/codex/src/app-server/config.ts` adds the new config enums and resolvers. - `extensions/codex/src/app-server/run-attempt.ts` selects the workspace prompt surface, computes the workspace fingerprint, chooses the positive SOUL bridge behavior, and keeps native project docs out of OpenClaw turn text. - `extensions/codex/src/app-server/thread-lifecycle.ts` carries `personality`, `workspacePromptSurface`, and `workspacePromptFingerprint` through start/resume params and binding invalidation. - `extensions/codex/src/app-server/session-binding.ts` persists the prompt-surface metadata. - Docs now describe the architecture in `docs/plugins/codex-harness-runtime.md`, `docs/plugins/codex-harness-reference.md`, and `docs/concepts/system-prompt.md`. ## Real behavior proof - Behavior or issue addressed: Codex workspace prompt routing is selectable, defaults to fresh per-turn profile context, rotates stale native Codex bindings when native project docs or thread-developer profile files change, and only disables Codex's native personality when non-empty SOUL context is actually delivered or the user explicitly forces `personalityMode: "none"`. - Current narrowed head tested: `ad037cc543` on branch `codex/workspace-context-routing-v2`, rebased on `upstream/main` `a13468320c`. - Scope note: this head removes the prior fs-safe default flip, archive fallback, Windows ACL policy change, and dependency expectation cleanup from the PR. The only non-Codex file left is `extensions/openrouter/provider-routing.ts`, a behavior-preserving object-spread lint cleanup required because the repo's fail-safe changed gate lints the extension surface on this branch. - Real environment tested: local OpenClaw checkout on macOS under `/Volumes/LEXAR/repos/openclaw-codex-workspace-routing`, using a temporary OpenClaw gateway and Codex app-server model route. - Exact steps or command run after this patch: ```bash OPENCLAW_LIVE=1 \ OPENCLAW_LIVE_CODEX_HARNESS=1 \ OPENCLAW_LIVE_CODEX_HARNESS_MODEL=codex/gpt-5.4-mini \ OPENCLAW_LIVE_CODEX_HARNESS_DEBUG=1 \ OPENCLAW_LIVE_CODEX_HARNESS_REQUEST_TIMEOUT_MS=300000 \ node scripts/test-live.mjs -- src/gateway/gateway-codex-harness.live.test.ts -t "project_doc_max_bytes" ``` - Evidence after fix: ```text [gateway-codex-live] project-doc-budget-binding-proof { "defaultHiddenEditKeptThread": true, "hiddenEditKeptThread": true, "visibleEditRotatedThread": true, "defaultFirst": { "threadId": "019e45f3-27a2-7f52-a28c-1cb464d20aa3", "workspacePromptFingerprint": "7040706c03c9a003b0d91a16dd33cb5dbe0f7e3c039e9042ed3e13b11765f15b", "workspacePromptSurface": "per_turn_context" }, "defaultHidden": { "threadId": "019e45f3-27a2-7f52-a28c-1cb464d20aa3", "workspacePromptFingerprint": "7040706c03c9a003b0d91a16dd33cb5dbe0f7e3c039e9042ed3e13b11765f15b", "workspacePromptSurface": "per_turn_context" }, "first": { "threadId": "019e45f3-4e9f-79d1-bb96-bfac5501bebb", "workspacePromptFingerprint": "67ae5644fa63c69b79a5bd91692a5a9eb276eb1921952c0f14aae55848cf9871", "workspacePromptSurface": "per_turn_context" }, "hidden": { "threadId": "019e45f3-4e9f-79d1-bb96-bfac5501bebb", "workspacePromptFingerprint": "67ae5644fa63c69b79a5bd91692a5a9eb276eb1921952c0f14aae55848cf9871", "workspacePromptSurface": "per_turn_context" }, "visible": { "threadId": "019e45f3-6951-7eb0-83ab-5b009ececa6f", "workspacePromptFingerprint": "1c20f3c6e3ba646eb74b03e4b75a176df0b1494bc095406685c7e6be74275508", "workspacePromptSurface": "per_turn_context" } } Test Files 1 passed (1) Tests 1 passed | 2 skipped (3) Duration 94.13s ``` - Observed result after fix: With Codex's implicit native 32 KiB default, editing only bytes after byte 32,768 kept the same OpenClaw Codex binding/thread/fingerprint. With `project_doc_max_bytes = 8`, editing only bytes outside the explicit native budget kept the same binding/thread/fingerprint, while editing bytes inside the native budget rotated both the native thread and workspace prompt fingerprint. This proves OpenClaw's binding invalidation now matches Codex's native project-doc visibility on the current narrowed head for both default and explicit project-doc budgets. - What was not tested: Broad cross-platform and full-matrix coverage are left to GitHub Actions; the live proof used local macOS Codex app-server auth/model access. ## Validation Current-head focused validation from `/Volumes/LEXAR/repos/openclaw-codex-workspace-routing`: ```bash OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 node scripts/run-vitest.mjs \ extensions/codex/src/app-server/run-attempt.test.ts \ -t "project_doc_max_bytes|default project-doc budget" ``` Result: 1 test file passed, 3 tests passed, 195 skipped. Current-head live Codex harness proof: ```bash OPENCLAW_LIVE=1 \ OPENCLAW_LIVE_CODEX_HARNESS=1 \ OPENCLAW_LIVE_CODEX_HARNESS_MODEL=codex/gpt-5.4-mini \ OPENCLAW_LIVE_CODEX_HARNESS_DEBUG=1 \ OPENCLAW_LIVE_CODEX_HARNESS_REQUEST_TIMEOUT_MS=300000 \ node scripts/test-live.mjs -- src/gateway/gateway-codex-harness.live.test.ts -t "project_doc_max_bytes" ``` Result: 1 test file passed, 1 test passed, 2 skipped. Current-head formatting/check validation: ```bash pnpm exec oxfmt --check \ extensions/codex/src/app-server/run-attempt.ts \ extensions/codex/src/app-server/run-attempt.test.ts \ src/gateway/gateway-codex-harness.live.test.ts git diff --check ``` Result: passed. Changed-gate validation: ```bash node scripts/run-oxlint-shards.mjs -- extensions/openrouter/provider-routing.ts pnpm check:changed ``` Result: passed. `pnpm check:changed` completed typecheck, lint, dependency/package guards, duplicate coverage, runtime sidecar loader guard, conflict-marker/changelog checks, and runtime import-cycle checks. ## Maintainer review notes This PR intentionally keeps the Codex-native lane narrow: - It does not change harness selection policy, model routing, auth routing, or Codex app-server transport behavior. - It preserves native Codex base/model instructions and native project-doc loading instead of replacing them with OpenClaw prompt text. - It keeps quality-first per-turn profile context as the default and makes token-efficient thread developer instructions opt-in. - It rotates native Codex bindings when the selected prompt surface, personality policy, visible native project docs, or `thread_developer` profile files change. - It leaves volatile `MEMORY.md`, active `BOOTSTRAP.md`, and skill context in the per-turn lane even when stable soul/profile files are opted into thread developer instructions.

target.title: fix(qwen): enable qwen3.6-plus on Coding Plan CN, correct reasoning flag
target.body:
## Summary

- **What changed**: Enables `qwen3.6-plus` on the DashScope Coding Plan CN endpoint (`coding.dashscope.aliyuncs.com`) and corrects its `reasoning` flag (`false` → `true`).
  - `extensions/qwen/models.ts`: `reasoning: false` → `reasoning: true` on the `qwen3.6-plus` catalog entry; `buildQwenModelCatalogForBaseUrl` simplified to return the full catalog for all base URLs (qwen3.6-plus no longer excluded from Coding Plan URLs).
  - `extensions/qwen/openclaw.plugin.json`: removes the two `modelCatalog.suppressions` entries that blocked `qwen3.6-plus` from Coding Plan configurations — confirmed available on `coding.dashscope.aliyuncs.com`.
  - `extensions/qwen/index.ts`: removes `normalizeConfig` (which stripped `qwen3.6-plus` from Coding Plan `providerConfig.models`); no longer needed after manifest suppression removal.
- **Why it matters**: `qwen3.6-plus` is available on `coding.dashscope.aliyuncs.com` (DashScope CN Coding Plan). The existing suppression incorrectly hid it from Coding Plan users. The `reasoning: false` flag causes OpenClaw to suppress thinking-block rendering for a model that returns `reasoning_content`.
- **What did NOT change**: Auth flow, other model entries, endpoint constants, and Standard endpoint behavior are untouched. Global Coding Plan (`coding-intl.dashscope.aliyuncs.com`) behavior is not claimed.

## Change Type (select all)

- [ ] Bug fix
- [x] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations (Qwen / DashScope provider catalog)
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Standalone catalog update; no linked issue.
- [ ] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- **Behavior addressed**: `qwen3.6-plus` was suppressed on Coding Plan CN and showed `reasoning: false`. After this patch it is available on `coding.dashscope.aliyuncs.com` and the reasoning flag is accurate.
- **Environment tested**: DashScope CN Coding Plan endpoint (`https://coding.dashscope.aliyuncs.com/v1`), live API key, direct HTTP.
- **Steps run after the patch**:
  1. Sent a completion request for `qwen3.6-plus` to `https://coding.dashscope.aliyuncs.com/v1` with a live Coding Plan API key.
  2. Inspected response for `reasoning_content` and `reasoning_tokens` confirming thinking-mode support.
- **Observed result**: `qwen3.6-plus` accepted on Coding Plan CN endpoint; `reasoning_content` and `reasoning_tokens` present in response, confirming `reasoning: true` is correct.
- **Evidence**:

<details>
<summary>Live response — qwen3.6-plus on coding.dashscope.aliyuncs.com</summary>

```text
POST https://coding.dashscope.aliyuncs.com/v1/chat/completions
Authorization: Bearer sk-<redacted>
```

```json
{
  "model": "qwen3.6-plus",
  "messages": [{"role": "user", "content": "What is 2+2? Answer in one word."}],
  "max_tokens": 50
}
```

```json
{
  "choices": [{
    "message": {
      "content": "Four",
      "reasoning_content": "Here's a thinking process:\n\n1. Question: \"What is 2+2?\"\n2. 2 + 2 = 4\n3. One word: Four",
      "role": "assistant"
    },
    "finish_reason": "stop",
    "index": 0
  }],
  "usage": {
    "prompt_tokens": 22,
    "completion_tokens": 227,
    "total_tokens": 249,
    "completion_tokens_details": {"reasoning_tokens": 221, "text_tokens": 227}
  },
  "model": "qwen3.6-plus"
}
```

`qwen3.6-plus` accepted on `coding.dashscope.aliyuncs.com`; `reasoning_content` and `reasoning_tokens` confirmed.

</details>

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? `No`
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? `No`
- New/changed network calls? `No`
- Command/tool execution surface changed? `No`
- Data access scope changed? `No`
- Risk summary: Catalog metadata change only. Removes an incorrect suppression; no new endpoint, auth flow, or runtime surface introduced.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- Endpoint: DashScope CN Coding Plan (`https://coding.dashscope.aliyuncs.com/v1`)
- Auth: DashScope Coding Plan API key (`sk-<redacted>`)

### Steps

1. POST completion request with `"model": "qwen3.6-plus"` to `https://coding.dashscope.aliyuncs.com/v1`.
2. Inspect response for `reasoning_content` and `completion_tokens_details.reasoning_tokens`.

### Expected

`qwen3.6-plus` responds on Coding Plan CN with `reasoning_content` present.

### Actual

Matches expected.

## Human Verification (required)

- **Verified scenarios**: `qwen3.6-plus` live on `coding.dashscope.aliyuncs.com`; `reasoning_content` present (flag corrected to `true`).
- **Edge cases checked**: None beyond CN Coding Plan endpoint.
- **What I did NOT verify**: Global Coding Plan endpoint (`coding-intl.dashscope.aliyuncs.com`) — not maintained by this contributor.

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [ ] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? `Yes` — removes a suppression; Coding Plan CN users who previously could not select `qwen3.6-plus` can now do so. No existing working configuration is broken.
- Config/env changes? `No`
- Migration needed? `No`

## Risks and Mitigations

- **Risk**: `qwen3.6-plus` may not be available on all Coding Plan CN accounts (account-level activation required).
  - Mitigation: Users without access will receive a vendor-side model-not-found error, same as any unavailable model; no crash or data loss.
- **Risk**: Global Coding Plan (`coding-intl.dashscope.aliyuncs.com`) behavior unknown — suppression removed for that endpoint too as a side effect.
  - Mitigation: If Global Coding Plan does not support `qwen3.6-plus`, affected users will see a vendor error. A follow-up can re-add a suppression scoped to `coding-intl` only if confirmed unavailable there.

target.title: Audit secret-bearing backup files
target.body:
## Summary
- extend `secrets audit` to scan sibling backup files for `.env`, `auth-profiles.json`, and generated `models.json`
- detect plaintext credential residue in `.bak` / `.bak.*` backup naming patterns without resolving historical backup-only SecretRefs
- keep `openclaw.json` config backup scanning out of this PR so it can remain aligned with #83275's config artifact scanner
- add coverage for env, auth profile, models backup residues, numbered backup files, and backup-only SecretRef cases

## Context
This addresses a remaining API-key exposure surface discussed in #11829: historical backup files can retain plaintext credentials even after users migrate active config to SecretRefs.

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior or issue addressed:** `openclaw secrets audit --json` now scans secret-bearing backup siblings for `.env`, `auth-profiles.json`, and generated `models.json`, and reports plaintext residues from those backup files. It does not scan `openclaw.json` config backups in this PR because #83275 owns that config-artifact surface.

**Real environment tested:** Local source checkout on macOS, Node.js v25.6.1, pnpm 11.1.0 via `npx pnpm@11.1.0`. The run used a temporary OpenClaw state directory with only redacted dummy values.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch:**
1. Created a temporary `OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR` with active `openclaw.json`, empty `auth-profiles.json`, and generated `models.json`.
2. Added redacted backup files: `.env.bak.1`, `auth-profiles.json.bak.1`, and `models.json.bak.1`.
3. Ran:

```bash
OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR="$TMP/state" \
OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH="$TMP/state/openclaw.json" \
OPENAI_API_KEY=sk-redacted-active \
npx pnpm@11.1.0 --silent openclaw secrets audit --json
```

**Evidence after fix:** Redacted console output from the real CLI run:

```json
{
  "status": "findings",
  "summary": {
    "plaintextCount": 3,
    "unresolvedRefCount": 0,
    "shadowedRefCount": 1,
    "legacyResidueCount": 0
  },
  "filesScanned": [
    "$TMP/state/.env.bak.1",
    "$TMP/state/agents/main/agent/auth-profiles.json",
    "$TMP/state/agents/main/agent/auth-profiles.json.bak.1",
    "$TMP/state/agents/main/agent/models.json",
    "$TMP/state/agents/main/agent/models.json.bak.1",
    "$TMP/state/openclaw.json"
  ],
  "backupFindings": [
    {
      "code": "PLAINTEXT_FOUND",
      "severity": "warn",
      "file": "$TMP/state/.env.bak.1",
      "jsonPath": "$env.OPENAI_API_KEY",
      "message": "Potential secret found in .env (OPENAI_API_KEY)."
    },
    {
      "code": "PLAINTEXT_FOUND",
      "severity": "warn",
      "file": "$TMP/state/agents/main/agent/auth-profiles.json.bak.1",
      "jsonPath": "profiles.openai:default.key",
      "message": "Auth profile API key is stored as plaintext."
    },
    {
      "code": "PLAINTEXT_FOUND",
      "severity": "warn",
      "file": "$TMP/state/agents/main/agent/models.json.bak.1",
      "jsonPath": "providers.openai.apiKey",
      "message": "models.json provider apiKey is stored as plaintext."
    }
  ]
}
```

**Observed result after fix:** The CLI returned `status: findings`, scanned all three backup files, and reported three backup plaintext findings with `unresolvedRefCount: 0`.

**What was not tested:** I did not test real user credentials, live providers, or `openclaw.json` config backup artifacts. The config backup artifact surface is intentionally left to #83275.

## Test
- `npx pnpm@11.1.0 vitest run src/secrets/audit.test.ts` -> 26 passed
- `npx pnpm@11.1.0 exec oxfmt --check src/secrets/storage-scan.ts src/secrets/audit.ts src/secrets/audit.test.ts`
- `npx pnpm@11.1.0 exec oxlint src/secrets/storage-scan.ts src/secrets/audit.ts src/secrets/audit.test.ts`
- `git diff --check`

target.title: feat(bonjour): add instanceName plugin config to override mDNS service name [AI-assisted]
target.body:
Closes #54467.

## Summary

Adds a `plugins.entries.bonjour.config.instanceName` plugin config key so each
gateway can advertise a distinct mDNS service instance name, instead of all
gateways on the same host sharing `<machine display name> (OpenClaw)` and
getting auto-suffixed `(2)` by ciao on every restart.

The advertiser already accepted an `opts.instanceName` override
(`extensions/bonjour/src/advertiser.ts:201-205`); only the plugin entry was
missing the config-driven path. Behavior with no config set is unchanged.

## Why this fits the plugin pattern (not `gateway.bonjour.*`)

The original issue suggested `gateway.bonjour.instanceName`, but per the
plugins boundary rules in `src/plugins/CLAUDE.md` ("do not normalize
plugin-owned into core-owned by scattering direct reads of
`plugins.entries.<id>.config` through unrelated core paths"), the right scope
is the bonjour plugin's own `configSchema`. Reads happen in
`extensions/bonjour/index.ts` via `api.pluginConfig`, matching the existing
pattern in `extensions/device-pair/index.ts:299`.

## Behavior

- `plugins.entries.bonjour.config.instanceName` unset → unchanged: uses
  `ctx.machineDisplayName`, suffixed `(OpenClaw)`.
- Set to `"AI VA PC"` → advertises as `AI VA PC (OpenClaw)`.
- Set to `"My Bot (OpenClaw)"` → advertises as `My Bot (OpenClaw)` (no
  double-suffix; existing `formatBonjourInstanceName` regex already handles
  this).
- Set to empty / whitespace → falls back to machine name.
- Non-string value → falls back to machine name (defensive).

## Repro (per #54467)

Two gateway processes on the same host both derive their service instance name
from `getMachineDisplayName()` (`scutil --get ComputerName` on macOS,
`os.hostname()` elsewhere). Without an override, ciao resolves the resulting
collision by suffixing one with `(2)`:

```
+   eth0 IPv4 mac-mini (OpenClaw)         _openclaw-gw._tcp    local
+   eth0 IPv4 mac-mini (OpenClaw) (2)     _openclaw-gw._tcp    local   ← ciao auto-rename
```

The reporter notes this can shift between instances on restart. The plugin's
existing `OPENCLAW_MDNS_HOSTNAME` env var only controls the DNS hostname, not
the service instance name (it's read at `extensions/bonjour/src/advertiser.ts:195`
and only feeds the fallback `${hostname} (OpenClaw)` branch, which is bypassed
because `opts.instanceName` is always non-empty after `formatBonjourInstanceName`).

## After

```jsonc
// gateway A's openclaw.json
{
  "plugins": {
    "entries": {
      "bonjour": {
        "config": { "instanceName": "Mac Mini Primary" }
      }
    }
  }
}

// gateway B's openclaw.json
{
  "plugins": {
    "entries": {
      "bonjour": {
        "config": { "instanceName": "Mac Mini Bot" }
      }
    }
  }
}
```

```
+   eth0 IPv4 Mac Mini Primary (OpenClaw)   _openclaw-gw._tcp    local
+   eth0 IPv4 Mac Mini Bot (OpenClaw)       _openclaw-gw._tcp    local
```

Each gateway is stably discoverable on its own name across restarts.

## Files

- `extensions/bonjour/openclaw.plugin.json` — add `instanceName` to
  `configSchema.properties`.
- `extensions/bonjour/index.ts` — read `api.pluginConfig.instanceName`,
  prefer it over `ctx.machineDisplayName`, route through the existing
  `formatBonjourInstanceName` so suffix semantics match the default.
- `extensions/bonjour/index.test.ts` — new plugin-entry test (5 cases:
  unset, empty/whitespace, configured-without-suffix, configured-with-suffix,
  defensive non-string).
- `docs/gateway/bonjour.md` — document the new config key alongside the
  existing disable/configure entries.
- `CHANGELOG.md` — single-line entry under `## 2026.4.25 (Unreleased)`.

## Tests

- `pnpm test extensions/bonjour` → 32/32 pass (4 files, includes the new 5).
- `pnpm check:changed` → green (typecheck + format + tests for affected lanes).
- `pnpm build` → ok.

## AI-assisted disclosure

This PR was authored with Claude (Anthropic). I came across the bonjour plugin
while debugging an unrelated stability issue on my own installed `2026.4.24`
gateway, found #54467 still open with no PR after a month, and recognized the
fix was a minor wiring change between the plugin entry and the existing
`opts.instanceName` advertiser path. Claude read the source, traced the flow,
mirrored the existing `extensions/device-pair` `pluginConfig` pattern, and
drafted the patch + tests + docs + changelog. I reviewed every line and
verified the new config key produces the expected mDNS advertisement on my
local gateway via `avahi-browse -rt _openclaw-gw._tcp`.

Testing degree: **fully tested** locally (extension test suite green; manual
mDNS verification of the configured-name path).
